\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}

\newcommand{\Aut}{\operatorname{Aut}}
\newcommand{\spanv}{\operatorname{span}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf A computably AUT-countable example with no finite existential
automorphism basis}
\end{center}

\begin{theorem}
There is a computably represented countable structure $\mathcal A$ such
that every automorphism of every copy of $\mathcal A$ is computable from
that copy, and hence $\mathcal A$ is computably AUT-countable on a cone,
but for every finite parameter set $P\subseteq A$ there is an
automorphism $\pi$ of $\mathcal A$ whose graph is not
$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable over $P\cup\pi(P)$.
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $V=[\omega]^{<\omega}$, with addition $+$ given by symmetric
difference; thus $V$ is a countably infinite vector space over
$\mathbb F_2$.  Fix a computable sequence $(D_i)_{i\in\omega}$ of finite
subsets of $V$ such that every finite subset of $V$ occurs cofinally
often.

We use a one-sorted computable coding of two sorts.  The domain is the
computable disjoint union
\[
        (\{0\}\times\omega)\ \sqcup\ (\{1\}\times V),
\]
identified effectively with $\omega$.  We write its two unary predicates
as $I$ and $S$.  The language also has a binary relation $<$ and a
ternary relation $R$.  On $I$ the relation $<$ is the usual order of
$\omega$, and $<$ is false if one of its arguments is outside $I$.  For
$i\in I$ and $x,y\in S$, put
\[
        R(i,x,y)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad x+y\in D_i,
\]
where we identify elements of the $S$-sort with vectors in $V$; on all
other triples $R$ is false.  This is a computable relational structure.

First compute its automorphism group.  Since $(I,<)$ has order type
$\omega$, every automorphism fixes $I$ pointwise.  If
$f\in\Aut(\mathcal A)$, let $c=f(0_V)$.  For every $h\in V$ choose $i$
with $D_i=\{h\}$.  Then $R(i,x,x+h)$ for all $x\in V$, and preservation
of $R$ gives $f(x+h)=f(x)+h$.  Taking $x=0_V$ gives
\[
        f(h)=c+h  \qquad(h\in V).
\]
Conversely, for each $c\in V$, the map $\tau_c$ which fixes $I$
pointwise and sends $x\in S$ to $x+c$ preserves all differences $x+y$,
hence preserves $R$.  Thus
\[
        \Aut(\mathcal A)=\{\tau_c:c\in V\}.
\]

This already implies computable AUT-countability, with no cone oracle.
Let $\mathcal B\cong\mathcal A$, and let $\sigma\in\Aut(\mathcal B)$.
Transporting $\sigma$ to $\mathcal A$, it is some translation $\tau_c$.
Choose an element $i$ of the $I$-sort of $\mathcal B$ corresponding to
some index with $D_i=\{c\}$.  A Turing program with oracle the atomic
diagram of $\mathcal B$ and with this natural number $i$ hardwired
computes $\sigma$: it queries the sort predicate, outputs $n$ on inputs
$n$ in the $I$-sort, and on inputs $x$ in the $S$-sort it searches for
the unique $y$ with $R(i,x,y)$.  Hence every automorphism of every copy
is computable relative to the copy.

It remains to prove the promised failure of $\Sigma^{in}_1$-definability.
Fix a finite parameter set $P\subseteq A$.  Let
\[
        M=\max(P\cap I)
\]
if $P\cap I\neq\varnothing$, and put $M=-1$ otherwise.  Let
\[
        W=\spanv\Bigl(\bigcup_{i\le M}D_i\Bigr)\le V
\]
(where $W=\{0\}$ if $M=-1$).  The subspace $W$ is finite.  Choose a
nonzero $h\in V\setminus W$, and let $\pi=\tau_h$.  Put
$Q=P\cup\pi(P)$.  Since the quotient $V/W$ is infinite and $Q\cap S$ is
finite, choose $x\in V$ such that the two cosets
\[
        x+W,\qquad x+h+W
\]
are disjoint from $Q\cap S$.  Let $y=x+h$.

We shall show that the graph-pair $(x,y)$ is not isolated inside
$\operatorname{graph}(\pi)$ by any existential formula over $Q$.  This
suffices, because if a countable disjunction of existential formulas
defined exactly the graph, then any disjunct true of $(x,y)$ would itself
have to be contained in the graph.

Let
\[
        \exists \bar z\,\theta(\bar q,u,v,\bar z)
\]
be an existential formula with parameters $\bar q$ from $Q$, and suppose
it is true at $(u,v)=(x,y)$.  Choose witnesses.  Passing to one true
conjunction of literals in a disjunctive normal form of the quantifier
free part, it is enough to preserve the truth values of the finitely many
literals in that conjunction.

Call an $I$-sort value \emph{low} if it is at most $M$, and
\emph{high} otherwise.  All low markers have their associated finite
sets $D_i$ contained in $W$.  Let $F$ be the finite set of all $S$-sort
elements occurring among $x,y$, the parameters, and the chosen witnesses,
and put $C=y+W$.  We shall add a vector $t$ precisely to those members of
$F$ which lie in $C$.  The coset $C$ contains no parameter and does not
contain $x$.

Choose $t\in V$ outside the following finite forbidden set.  First avoid
$t=0$ and all choices causing a shifted element of $F\cap C$ to collide
with an unshifted element of $F\setminus C$.  Also, for every
$i\le M$, every $a\in F\cap C$, every $b\in F\setminus C$, and every
$d\in D_i$, avoid $t=a+b+d$.  These exclusions guarantee that all
equalities and all $R$-literals whose first argument is a low marker are
preserved: within $C$ all differences are unchanged; outside $C$ nothing
is changed; and across the cut the original difference is not in $W$,
while the new difference is kept out of each low $D_i$.

There are also finitely many high $I$-sort equality classes among the
chosen witnesses.  For each such class $\mu$, and for each pair
consisting of a positive and a negative $R$-literal with first argument
in $\mu$ and other two arguments in the $S$-sort, we additionally require
that the two resulting differences after the vector shift be unequal.
If the two differences are shifted in the same way this inequality
already follows from the original true diagram; otherwise it excludes
one further value of $t$.  Thus only finitely many more values are
forbidden, and we choose $t$ avoiding all of them.

Now perform the vector shift by this $t$.  For each high equality class
$\mu$, let $A_\mu$ be the finite set of new differences required by the
positive $R$-literals with first argument in $\mu$, and let $B_\mu$ be
the finite set of new differences forbidden by the negative such
literals.  By the choice of $t$, $A_\mu\cap B_\mu=\varnothing$.  Replace
the high $I$-sort witnesses by new indices, all above $M$ and in the
same equality and order pattern as before, with associated sets exactly
$A_\mu$.  This is possible because each finite subset of $V$ occurs
cofinally often among the $D_i$'s.  Sort predicates, equalities, and
$<$-literals are preserved by construction.  Literals involving $R$ with
an argument in the wrong sort remain false, low-marker $R$-literals were
handled above, and high-marker $R$-literals are made true or false
according as their resulting difference lies in $A_\mu$ or not.

Consequently the same existential formula is true of the new pair
\[
        (x,\;y+t)=(x,\;x+h+t).
\]
But $t\neq0$, so $y+t\neq y=\pi(x)$.  Hence this new pair is not in the
graph of $\pi$.  Therefore no existential formula over
$Q=P\cup\pi(P)$ which is true of $(x,\pi(x))$ can be contained in the
graph of $\pi$, and the graph of $\pi$ is not
$\Sigma^{in}_1$-definable over $Q$.

Since $P$ was arbitrary, the required property holds for every finite
parameter set.
\end{proof}

\end{document}

\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb R}
\newcommand{\Z}{\mathbb Z}
\newcommand{\ellg}{\operatorname{length}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf The infimum is \(\sqrt3\)}
\end{center}

Let \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\).  This is a compact flat
Mobius band, with boundary a circle of length \(2\beta\).  If \(\beta\)
is realized, condition (2) says exactly that the given map is constant on
the \(G_\beta\)-orbits and separates distinct orbits.  Hence it descends
to a continuous injection \(F:M_\beta\to\R^3\), and this injection is an
embedding because \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\R^3\) is Hausdorff.  The
locally finite invariant triangulation descends to a locally finite
triangulation of the compact surface \(M_\beta\), hence to a finite one.

In a clean triangle, call the boundary edge the ridge and the opposite
vertex the apex.  A bend is a segment in the triangle joining the apex to
a point of the ridge.

\begin{lemma}[bends lengthen]\label{lem:lengthen}
Let \(L\) be the affine map on one clean triangle.  If \(P\) is any point
of the ridge and \(C\) is the apex, then
\[
        |L(P)-L(C)|>|P-C|.
\]
Moreover every non-degenerate boundary subarc is strictly shortened by
\(F\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let the ridge endpoints be \(A,B\), and put \(P_s=(1-s)A+sB\).  The
function
\[
 q(s)= |L(P_s)-L(C)|^2-|P_s-C|^2
\]
satisfies
\[
 q''(s)=2\big(|L(A)-L(B)|^2-|A-B|^2\big)<0,
\]
because the ridge is shortened.  Since the two side edges are lengthened,
\(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\).  Concavity gives \(q(s)>0\) for
\(0\le s\le1\).  On each boundary edge the affine map has constant
Lipschitz factor \(<1\); an arbitrary boundary subarc is split at the
finitely many boundary vertices it meets, and the strict inequalities add.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[a polygonal \(T\)-pair]\label{lem:T}
For \(F(M_\beta)\) there are two bends whose image segments lie on
perpendicular intersecting lines.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The bends form a circle.  Indeed, every bend meets the center circle
\(\{y=1/2\}/\langle G_\beta\rangle\) once, and every point of the center
circle lies in a unique bend; on a common non-boundary edge the two
adjacent triangles give the same limiting bend.

Parametrize this bend circle by \(u\in\R/\Z\), lift to \(u\in\R\), and
choose the image direction \(e(u)\) from the lower side of the lifted
strip to the upper side.  Let \(m(u)\) be the image midpoint.  The
previous lemma makes every image bend non-degenerate, and the finite
polygonal structure gives continuous functions \(e,m\).  Going once
around the Mobius band reverses the chosen orientation, so
\[
        e(u+1)=-e(u),\qquad m(u+1)=m(u).
\]
For \(0<r<1\) define
\[
 \Phi(u,r)=\Big(e(u)\!\cdot e(u+r),\,
 (m(u)-m(u+r))\!\cdot (e(u)\times e(u+r))\Big).
\]
It is periodic in \(u\).  As \(r\to0\) it tends to \((1,0)\), and as
\(r\to1\) it tends to \((-1,0)\).  Thus it extends continuously to the
two-point compactification of \(S^1\times(0,1)\), obtained by collapsing
the two boundary circles of \(S^1\times[0,1]\); this compactification is
\(S^2\).

The involution \(\iota(u,r)=(u+r,1-r)\) interchanges the two bends.  Under
the coordinates
\[
(\sin\pi r\cos 2\pi(u+r/2),\,\sin\pi r\sin 2\pi(u+r/2),\,\cos\pi r)
\]
on the compactified cylinder, \(\iota\) is the antipodal map.  From
\(e(u+1)=-e(u)\) and \(m(u+1)=m(u)\) one checks that
\(\Phi\circ\iota=-\Phi\).  Borsuk--Ulam gives a zero of \(\Phi\).

At such a zero the two direction vectors are perpendicular.  The second
coordinate says that the vector joining the two midpoints lies in the
plane spanned by those directions; hence the two supporting lines are
coplanar.  Since they are not parallel, they intersect.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:lower}
Every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Choose the two bends from Lemma \ref{lem:T}.  Their image segments have
disjoint relative interiors, because \(F\) is an embedding.  Let \(O\) be
the intersection of their supporting lines.  At least one image segment
does not contain \(O\) in its relative interior; call that bend \(B\), and
call the other one \(T\).

Lift \(T\) to a segment from \((0,0)\) to \((t,1)\).  Replacing \(x\) by
\(-x\), if necessary, we may assume \(t\ge0\).  In fact \(t<\beta\): if
\(t>\beta\), this lifted segment meets its \(G_\beta\)-translate in its
interior, and if \(t=\beta\), its endpoints are \(G_\beta\)-equivalent.
Either case contradicts the fact that the image of a bend is a
non-degenerate affine segment and that \(F\) is embedded on the quotient.

Cutting \(M_\beta\) along \(T\) gives two boundary arcs \(H,D\) of lengths
\[
        \ellg(H)=\beta-t,\qquad \ellg(D)=\beta+t.        \tag{1}
\]
Indeed the upper endpoint \((t,1)\) has boundary coordinate \(t+\beta\)
modulo \(2\beta\).  Let \(T^*=F(T)\).  Since \(H^*=F(H)\) joins the
endpoints of \(T^*\), while boundary arcs are shortened and bends are
lengthened,
\[
        \beta-t=\ellg(H)>\ellg(H^*)\ge |T^*|>\sqrt{1+t^2}.       \tag{2}
\]

The line of \(B^*\) is perpendicular to the line of \(T^*\).  Since \(O\)
is not in the relative interior of \(B^*\), one endpoint \(v^*\) of
\(B^*\) has distance from the line of \(T^*\) at least \(|B^*|\).  The
domain length of \(B\) is at least \(1\), so Lemma \ref{lem:lengthen}
gives \(|B^*|>1\).  Let \(A\) be the boundary arc, either \(H\) or \(D\),
which contains the corresponding boundary endpoint \(v\).

The image arc \(A^*\) goes from one endpoint of \(T^*\) to the other
through \(v^*\).  If a point has perpendicular distance \(h\) from a line
segment of length \(L\), the sum of its distances to the two endpoints is
at least \(\sqrt{L^2+4h^2}\), with equality only over the midpoint.  Here
\(L=|T^*|>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(h>1\), so
\[
        \ellg(A)>\ellg(A^*)\ge
        \hbox{(that sum)}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.                  \tag{3}
\]

If \(A=H\), then \(D\ge H\) and (3) gives
\[
        2\beta=H+D>2\sqrt{5+t^2}>2\sqrt3.
\]
Thus assume \(A=D\).  From (1)--(3),
\[
 2\beta=H+D>\sqrt{1+t^2}+\sqrt{5+t^2},                         \tag{4}
\]
and also
\[
 2\beta=2D-2t>2\sqrt{5+t^2}-2t.                                \tag{5}
\]
Let
\[
 a(t)=\sqrt{1+t^2}+\sqrt{5+t^2},\qquad
 b(t)=2\sqrt{5+t^2}-2t .
\]
On \([0,\infty)\), \(a\) is increasing and \(b\) is decreasing, and
\[
        a(1/\sqrt3)=b(1/\sqrt3)=2\sqrt3 .
\]
Hence \(\max(a(t),b(t))\ge2\sqrt3\) for all \(t\ge0\).  Equations
(4)--(5) imply \(2\beta>2\sqrt3\).
\end{proof}

It remains to construct realizations for all larger \(\beta\).

\begin{lemma}[an embedded six-triangle model]\label{lem:model}
Let \(E_0E_1E_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side \(a\).  For every
sufficiently small \(\varepsilon>0\) there are points \(C_i(\varepsilon)\)
tending to the midpoint of \(E_iE_{i+1}\) (indices mod \(3\)) such that
the six affine triangles
\[
(E_i,C_i,E_{i+2}),\qquad (C_i,E_{i+1},E_{i+2}),\qquad i=0,1,2,
\]
form an embedded polyhedral Mobius band with boundary
\(E_0C_0E_1C_1E_2C_2E_0\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Embeddedness is preserved by nonsingular affine changes of coordinates,
so it is enough to verify one model.  Take
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
A_0=(0,0,0),& A_1=(1,0,0),& A_2=(0,1,0),\\
B_0=(1/2,0,\varepsilon),&
B_1=(1/2-\varepsilon,1/2,-2\varepsilon),&
B_2=(\varepsilon,1/2,0),
\end{array}
\]
where \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\), and take the six faces
\[
\Delta_0=A_0B_0A_2,\quad \Delta_1=B_0A_1A_2,\quad
\Delta_2=A_1B_1A_0,\quad \Delta_3=B_1A_2A_0,
\]
\[
\Delta_4=A_2B_2A_1,\quad \Delta_5=B_2A_0A_1 .
\]
The links of the vertices are intervals.  For instance
\[
 \operatorname{lk}(A_i)=
 B_i-A_{i+2}-B_{i+1}-A_{i+1}-B_{i+2},\qquad
 \operatorname{lk}(B_i)=A_i-A_{i+2}-A_{i+1}
\]
(indices mod \(3\), and reversal of a path is immaterial).  Thus the
abstract complex is a connected compact surface.  Its single boundary
cycle is \(A_0B_0A_1B_1A_2B_2A_0\), and
\(\chi=6-12+6=0\); hence, by the classification of compact surfaces, it
is a Mobius band once its geometric realization is embedded.

It remains to check embeddedness.  The face normals are
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
n_0=(-\varepsilon,0,1/2),&
n_1=(\varepsilon,\varepsilon,1/2),&
n_2=(0,2\varepsilon,1/2),\\
n_3=(2\varepsilon,0,1/2-\varepsilon),&
n_4=(0,0,1/2-\varepsilon),&
n_5=(0,0,1/2).
\end{array}
\]
For the edge-sharing pairs
\((0,1),(0,3),(1,4),(2,3),(2,5)\) these normals are not proportional, so
the two planes meet in the line of the common edge and the two triangles
meet exactly in that edge.  The remaining edge-sharing pair is
\((4,5)\).  These two triangles are coplanar in \(z=0\), but their third
vertices lie on opposite sides of their common edge \(B_2A_1\), since
\[
 \det(A_1-B_2,A_2-B_2)=\frac{1-2\varepsilon}{2}>0,\qquad
 \det(A_1-B_2,A_0-B_2)=-\frac{1}{2}<0 .
\]
Therefore \(\Delta_4\cap\Delta_5=B_2A_1\).

The other nine pairs of faces share only one vertex.  For each of them,
the normals listed above are not proportional, so the two planes meet in a
line through that vertex.  Expressing one direction of this line in the two edge bases
at the shared vertex gives the following sign table:
\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
(i,j)&\Delta_i&\Delta_j\\ \hline
(0,2)&(-,+)&(-,+)\\
(0,4)&(-,0)&(-,+)\\
(0,5)&(0,-)&(-,+)\\
(1,2)&(-,+)&(+,-)\\
(1,3)&(+,-)&(-,+)\\
(1,5)&(0,+)&(+,-)\\
(2,4)&(0,-)&(+,-)\\
(3,4)&(0,-)&(-,+)\\
(3,5)&(0,+)&(+,-)
\end{array}
\]
for \(0<\varepsilon<1/4\).  For example, for the pair \((0,2)\)
one line direction is \((-\varepsilon,\varepsilon/2,-2\varepsilon^2)\),
with cone coordinates
\((-2\varepsilon,\varepsilon/2)\) and
\((\varepsilon(2\varepsilon-3)/2,\varepsilon)\).  The other entries are
obtained by the same two-by-two linear calculation; zeros occur only
where displayed.  Call a sign pair non-negative (respectively
non-positive) when both of its entries are so.  In no row are the two
sign pairs both non-negative or both non-positive.  Therefore neither direction of the intersection line
lies in both closed tangent cones, and the two closed triangles meet only
at their common vertex.  This proves the model is embedded.  Applying the affine map which sends \(A_i\) to \(E_i\) gives
the stated points \(C_i(\varepsilon)\), and they tend to the side
midpoints as \(\varepsilon\to0\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[realizations above \(\sqrt3\)]\label{lem:upper}
Every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Put \(\delta=\beta/3\).  Choose \(a\) with
\[
        \max\{2/\sqrt3,\sqrt{1+\delta^2}\}<a<2\delta ;
\]
such an \(a\) exists because \(\delta>1/\sqrt3\).

Triangulate \(M_\beta\) in the fundamental rectangle
\([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\), with \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\).  Put
\[
\begin{array}{c}
A_0=(0,0),\quad B_0=(\delta,0),\quad A_1=(2\delta,0),\quad B_1=(\beta,0),\\
B_1=(0,1),\quad A_2=(\delta,1),\quad B_2=(2\delta,1),\quad A_0=(\beta,1).
\end{array}
\]
Split the three rectangles of width \(\delta\) by the diagonals
\(A_0A_2\), \(A_1A_2\), and \(A_1A_0\), respectively.  Equivalently, the
six faces are
\[
(A_i,B_i,A_{i+2}),\qquad (B_i,A_{i+1},A_{i+2}),\qquad i=0,1,2
\]
(indices mod \(3\)).  This is a clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\), and
its lift to \(\Sigma\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Use Lemma \ref{lem:model} with side length \(a\), and choose
\(\varepsilon\) so small that the following strict inequalities, which
hold in the limit \(\varepsilon\to0\), still hold:
\[
 |E_iC_i|<\delta,\qquad |C_iE_{i+1}|<\delta,\qquad
 |C_iE_{i+2}|>1 .
\]
Also \(|E_iE_j|=a>\sqrt{1+\delta^2}\).  Map the six vertices
\(A_i,B_i\) to \(E_i,C_i\), respectively, and extend affinely over each
of the six triangles.  Lemma \ref{lem:model} gives an embedding
\(F:M_\beta\to\R^3\).

Each ridge in the domain has length \(\delta\), and its image has length
\(<\delta\).  Each non-boundary edge is of one of two types: either it has
domain length \(1\), and image length \(|C_iE_{i+2}|>1\), or it has domain
length \(\sqrt{1+\delta^2}\), and image length \(a>\sqrt{1+\delta^2}\).
Thus \(F\) is a squeeze map on every triangle.  Lifting \(F\) to
\(\Sigma\) gives a continuous map invariant under \(G_\beta\); since
\(F\) is an embedding of the quotient, the lifted map satisfies condition
(2).  Hence \(\beta\) is realized.
\end{proof}

Proposition \ref{prop:lower} shows that realized numbers are all
\(>\sqrt3\), while Lemma \ref{lem:upper} realizes every \(\beta>\sqrt3\).
Therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

\end{document}

\newcommand{\Ber}{\operatorname{Bernoulli}}
\newcommand{\conv}{\operatorname{conv}}

\begin{document}

\title{A weighted Bernoulli mean-tail inequality: partial resolution and reductions}
\author{}
\date{}
\maketitle

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret the question in the universal sense: determine those \(p\in[0,1]\)
for which
\[
        \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^m w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p                  \tag{P}
\]
holds for every \(m\ge1\) and every non-negative probability vector
\((w_1,\ldots,w_m)\), with \(v_i\) i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).

The counterexamples in Proposition~\ref{prop:negative} show that no value
outside
\[
        [0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}
\]
can work.  Conversely, a full classification would follow from the
small-\(p\) assertion (Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}) that all
\(0\le p\le1/3\) work.  That assertion remains the only essential gap in this
draft.  What is proved unconditionally below is the complete negative direction,
several infinite positive families, closure properties, equivalent forms of the
remaining assertion, and a coalescence route that is ruled out by explicit
examples.

\section*{Positive values proved here}

Let \(\mathcal G\) be the set of probabilities for which (P) holds universally.

\begin{proposition}[a coloring criterion]\label{prop:criterion}
Let \(p=a/b\), where \(1\le a\le b\) are integers.  Suppose that every
non-negative \(b\)-tuple \(x_1,\ldots,x_b\) with \(\sum_j x_j=1\) has at least
\[
        \frac{a}{b}\binom ba=\binom{b-1}{a-1}
\]
\(a\)-subsets \(J\subseteq[b]\) satisfying \(\sum_{j\in J}x_j\ge a/b\).  Then
\(a/b\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Randomly color each original index \(i\) independently and uniformly with one
of \(b\) colors, and let \(X_j\) be the total weight of color \(j\).  For every
coloring outcome, the hypothesis gives at least \(\binom{b-1}{a-1}\) good
\(a\)-subsets of colors.  Hence, for a uniformly random \(a\)-subset \(J\) of
colors,
\[
       \Pr\!\left[\sum_{j\in J}X_j\ge a/b\right]\ge
       \frac{\binom{b-1}{a-1}}{\binom ba}=\frac ab .
\]
By symmetry the same lower bound holds for any fixed \(a\)-subset \(J\).  For
such a fixed \(J\), membership of an original index in \(J\) is an independent
\(\Ber(a/b)\) event, which is exactly (P).
\end{proof}

The criterion immediately gives the reciprocal points \(p=1/k\), \(k\ge3\):
among \(k\) color classes at least one has weight at least \(1/k\).

It also gives the following second family.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:pairs}
Let \(b\ge6\), and let \(x_1,\ldots,x_b\ge0\) with \(\sum_i x_i=1\).  Then at
least \(b-1\) unordered pairs \(\{i,j\}\) satisfy
\[
       x_i+x_j\ge \frac2b .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Put \(z_i=bx_i/2\).  Then \(\sum_i z_i=b/2\), and we must find at least
\(b-1\) pairs with \(z_i+z_j\ge1\).

Maintain a remaining set \(R\) of size \(s\) and sum at least \(s/2\).  Let
\(u\) and \(a\) be the smallest and largest elements of \(R\).  Since the
average of the elements of \(R\) is at least \(1/2\), its largest element
satisfies \(a\ge1/2\).  If \(u+a\ge1\), then \(a\) makes good pairs with all
other \(s-1\) elements and we stop.  Otherwise \(u+a<1\).  Let \(h\) be the
number of other elements making a good pair with \(a\).  If \(h\le(s-2)/2\),
then the \(h\) partners of \(a\) are at most \(a\), while the other \(s-1-h\)
elements are \(<1-a\), so
\[
 \sum_{z\in R}z
   < a+ha+(s-1-h)(1-a)
   =s-1-h+a(2h-s+2)\le s/2.
\]
The last inequality uses \(a\ge1/2\) and \(2h-s+2\le0\).  This contradiction
shows that \(h\ge\lfloor s/2\rfloor\).  Count these \(h\) pairs, delete \(u\)
and \(a\), and continue.  The remaining sum is
\(>s/2-1=(s-2)/2\), so the invariant is preserved.  Pairs counted at different
stages are distinct.  If the process reaches two remaining elements, their sum is
at least \(1\) by the invariant, so this final pair is counted and the process
stops; if one element remains, no further pair is available.  These terminal
possibilities are included in the ``never stops early'' count below.

If the procedure never stops before the terminal case, then for \(b=2r\) it
counts
\(r+(r-1)+\cdots+1\ge2r-1=b-1\), and for \(b=2r+1\) it counts
\(r+(r-1)+\cdots+1\ge2r=b-1\), using \(r\ge3\).

If it stops after \(t\) deletion steps, then \(s=b-2t\) elements remain and the
number counted is
\[
       \sum_{i=0}^{t-1}\left\lfloor \frac{b-2i}{2}\right\rfloor+(b-2t-1).
\]
For \(b=2r\) this exceeds \(b-1\) by
\[
       \frac{t(2r-t-3)}{2}\ge0\qquad(0\le t\le r-1,\ r\ge3),
\]
and for \(b=2r+1\) the same displayed expression is the excess over \(b-1\).
Hence at least \(b-1\) good pairs are always counted.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:positive}
The desired inequality holds for \(p=0\), for \(p=1/k\) with every integer
\(k\ge3\), for \(p=2/b\) with every integer \(b\ge6\), for \(p=1/2\), and for
\(p=1\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The cases \(p=0,1\) are immediate.  The reciprocal cases follow from
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} with \(a=1\), and Lemma~\ref{lem:pairs} plus
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} gives \(p=2/b\), \(b\ge6\).

For \(p=1/2\), put \(X=\sum_i w_i v_i\).  Since \(1-v_i\) has the same law as
\(v_i\), \(X\) and \(1-X\) have the same distribution.  Hence
\(\Pr[X<1/2]=\Pr[X>1/2]\), and so \(\Pr[X\ge1/2]\ge1/2\).
\end{proof}

A possible pointwise verification of the hypothesis of
Proposition~\ref{prop:criterion} would ask that, for every non-negative
\(b\)-tuple of total \(1\), at least \(\binom{b-1}{a-1}\) of the
\(a\)-subsets have load at least \(a/b\).  This deterministic statement is
false already at \(a/b=3/10\): take seven
loads equal to \(1/7\) and three loads equal to \(0\).  A triple is then heavy
only if all three of its points come from the seven positive positions, giving
\(\binom73=35<36=\binom92\).  Thus the missing rational cases require genuinely
averaged information about the random coloring, not just a pointwise
Manickam--Miklos--Singhi type count.

\begin{proposition}[multiplicative closure]\label{prop:closure}
If \(p,q\in\mathcal G\), then \(pq\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
If \(pq=0\), the conclusion is trivial.  Otherwise generate independent
\(\Ber(pq)\) variables as \(v_i=x_i y_i\), where
\(x_i\) are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\), \(y_i\) are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(q)\), and the two
families are independent.  Put \(T=\sum_i w_i x_i\).  With probability at least
\(p\), \(T\ge p\).  Conditional on such an outcome, the normalized weights
\(w_i x_i/T\) form a probability vector, and the \(q\)-inequality gives
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i \frac{w_i x_i}{T}y_i\ge q\,\middle|\,x_1,\ldots,x_m\right]
 \ge q .
\]
On this event \(\sum_i w_i x_i y_i\ge qT\ge pq\), proving the claim.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[closure of the set of good probabilities]\label{prop:closed}
The set \(\mathcal G\) is closed in \([0,1]\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_n\in\mathcal G\) and \(p_n\to p\).  Fix a finite weight vector \(w\).
For \(r\in[0,1]\) put
\[
        F(r)=\Pr_r\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge r\right],
\]
where the subscript denotes the Bernoulli parameter.  There are only finitely
many subset sums \(w(A)\).

If infinitely many \(p_n\le p\), pass to that subsequence.  Among the finitely
many subset sums strictly smaller than \(p\), let \(p-\delta\) be the largest
one, if it exists; otherwise take any \(\delta>0\).  For all large \(n\),
\(p_n>p-\delta\), so no subset sum lies in \([p_n,p)\).  Hence the
event \(\{w(A)\ge p_n\}\) is eventually the same family of subsets as
\(\{w(A)\ge p\}\).  Therefore \(F(p)=\lim F(p_n)\ge\lim p_n=p\).

If instead infinitely many \(p_n>p\), pass to that subsequence.  Let \(p+\delta\)
be the smallest subset sum strictly larger than \(p\), if it exists; otherwise
take any \(\delta>0\).  For all large \(n\), \(p_n<p+\delta\), so the event
\(\{w(A)\ge p_n\}\) is the fixed family \(\{w(A)>p\}\), and
\[
       \mu_p\{A:w(A)>p\}=\lim F(p_n)\ge p .
\]
Since \(\{w(A)>p\}\subseteq\{w(A)\ge p\}\), again \(F(p)\ge p\).  As the weight
vector was arbitrary, \(p\in\mathcal G\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}\label{cor:base}
If the inequality can be proved for all \(p\in[1/6,1/3]\), then it holds for
all \(p\in[0,1/3]\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
For \(0<p\le1/3\), choose \(n\ge0\) such that \(2^n p\in[1/6,1/3]\).  Since
\(1/2\in\mathcal G\), repeated use of Proposition~\ref{prop:closure} gives
\(p=(2^n p)2^{-n}\in\mathcal G\).  The point \(p=0\) is trivial.
\end{proof}

\section*{Values that cannot work}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:negative}
The desired universal inequality fails for every
\(p\in(1/3,1/2)\cup(1/2,1)\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
If \(1/3<p<1/2\), take \(m=3\) and \(w_1=w_2=w_3=1/3\).  At least two
successes are needed, so
\[
\Pr\!\left[\frac{v_1+v_2+v_3}{3}\ge p\right]
  =3p^2(1-p)+p^3
  =3p^2-2p^3 .
\]
But \(3p^2-2p^3-p=p(1-p)(2p-1)<0\) on \(1/3<p<1/2\).

If \(1/2<p<1\), take \(m=2\) and \(w_1=w_2=1/2\).  The event requires two
successes and has probability \(p^2<p\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Equivalent forms of the remaining small-\(p\) assertion}

\begin{conjecture}[small-\(p\) assertion]\label{conj:missing}
If \(0\le p\le1/3\), \(w_i\ge0\), \(\sum_iw_i=1\), and the \(v_i\)'s are
i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\), then
\[
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p .
\]
\end{conjecture}

The assertion is sharp even in finite examples: if one weight is at least \(p\) and
the sum of all remaining weights is \(<p\), then the event in (P) is exactly
the event that this large coordinate succeeds, and its probability is \(p\).

The nontrivial sharpness mechanism with all weights below \(p\) is a pivot plus
dust.  Fix \(0<p\le1/3\), choose \(0<\varepsilon<p\), put
\(a=p-\varepsilon\), and take
\[
   w_0=a,\qquad
   w_1=\cdots=w_N=\frac{1-a}{N}.
\]
For all sufficiently large \(N\), the dust weights \((1-a)/N\), as well as the
pivot \(a\), are below \(p\).
If \(Y_N=(1-a)N^{-1}{\rm Bin}(N,p)\), then
\[
 \Pr\!\left[w_0v_0+Y_N\ge p\right]
 =
 p\,\Pr[Y_N\ge\varepsilon]+(1-p)\Pr[Y_N\ge p].
\]
Since \(Y_N\to p(1-a)=p(1-p+\varepsilon)\) in probability and
\(\varepsilon<p(1-p+\varepsilon)<p\), this probability tends to \(p\).

A particularly useful normalization is the following ``unit-gap'' form.

\begin{proposition}[unit-gap form]\label{prop:unitgap}
For a fixed \(0<p\le1/3\), Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent to the
following assertion.  Let \(q=1-p\), let \(0\le b_i\le1\),
\(B=\sum_i b_i\), and let \(\zeta_i\) be i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).  Then
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\ge p(B+1)\right]
 \ge
 \frac{p}{q}\,
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i<p(B+1)-1\right].            \tag{UG}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Assume Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.  Apply it to the normalized coefficient vector
\((1,b_1,\ldots,b_n)/(B+1)\), and condition on the
Bernoulli variable multiplying the coefficient \(1\).  With
\(S=\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\),
\[
 p\,\Pr[S\ge p(B+1)-1]+q\,\Pr[S\ge p(B+1)]\ge p,
\]
which rearranges to (UG).

Conversely, suppose (UG) is known.  If an original weight is at least \(p\),
then that coordinate alone proves (P).  Otherwise choose a pivot of maximal
weight \(a\in(0,p)\).  Denote the residual weights by \(u_i\), and write
\(u_i=a b_i\).  Then \(0\le b_i\le1\) and \(a(B+1)=1\).  Multiplying (UG) by \(a\) in the thresholds
gives
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]
 \ge \frac pq
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i<p-a\right].
\]
Conditioning on the pivot variable then yields
\[
 p\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p-a\right]
 +q\,\Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]\ge p,
\]
which is (P).
\end{proof}

The same statement will sometimes be more convenient in the following
homogeneous form.  For every \(h>0\), every \(0\le b_i\le h\), and
\(B=\sum_i b_i\),
\[
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\ge p(B+h)\right]
 \ge
 \frac{p}{q}\,
 \Pr\!\left[\sum_i b_i\zeta_i<p(B+h)-h\right].            \tag{HUG}
\]
Indeed, dividing all coefficients by \(h\) reduces (HUG) to (UG), and \(h=1\)
recovers (UG).  Thus Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}, (UG), and (HUG) are
equivalent for each fixed \(0<p\le1/3\), with \(p=0\) trivial.

The following shifted version is one rigorous piece of the natural induction.
It does not cover the pivot normalization needed for the full conjecture, but it
identifies exactly where the one-coordinate induction breaks.

\begin{proposition}[subcritical shifted unit-gap inequality]\label{prop:subcritical}
Let \(0<p\le1/3\), \(q=1-p\), \(h>0\), \(0\le b_i\le h\), \(B=\sum_i b_i\),
and \(S=\sum_i b_i\zeta_i\), where the \(\zeta_i\)'s are i.i.d.\ \(\Ber(p)\).
For
\[
        0\le \delta\le qh-pB
\]
one has
\[
 q\,\Pr\!\left[S\ge p(B+h)+\delta\right]
 \ge
 p\,\Pr\!\left[S<p(B+h)+\delta-h\right].                 \tag{6}
\]
In particular (HUG) is proved whenever \(B\le qh/p\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
We induct on the number of coefficients.  If there are none, then
\(B=0\), and for \(0\le\delta\le qh\) both events in (6) are empty (with the
left threshold positive and the right threshold non-positive).

Write \(S=R+c\zeta\), \(B=B'+c\), with \(0\le c\le h\).  Put
\(T=p(B+h)+\delta\).  The difference between the two sides of (6) is
\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
& q\Pr[S\ge T]-p\Pr[S<T-h]                                      \\
&\quad =
q\Big(q\Pr[R\ge T]-p\Pr[R<T-h]\Big)
+p\Big(q\Pr[R\ge T-c]-p\Pr[R<T-h-c]\Big).
\end{aligned}                                                   \tag{7}
\end{equation}
For the first bracket, observe that
\[
        T=p(B'+h)+(\delta+pc),
\]
and
\[
        0\le\delta+pc\le qh-pB+pc=qh-pB'.
\]
Thus the induction hypothesis applies to \(R\) with shift \(\delta+pc\).

For the second bracket,
\[
        T-c=p(B'+h)+(\delta-qc).
\]
If \(\delta-qc\ge0\), then
\[
        \delta-qc\le\delta\le qh-pB\le qh-pB',
\]
so induction applies again.  If \(\delta-qc<0\), then
\(T-c<p(B'+h)\) and \(T-h-c<p(B'+h)-h\).  Hence
\[
 \Pr[R\ge T-c]\ge \Pr[R\ge p(B'+h)]
\]
and
\[
 \Pr[R<T-h-c]\le \Pr[R<p(B'+h)-h].
\]
The induction hypothesis at shift \(0\) therefore makes the second bracket
non-negative.  Both brackets in (7) are non-negative, completing the induction.
\end{proof}

For rational parameters there is also a useful exact cyclic formulation.  The
case \(p=0\) is trivial, so let \(p=r/b\le1/3\) with \(1\le r\le b\), in
lowest or non-lowest terms.  Assign each coefficient \(w_i\)
independently and uniformly to one of the \(b\) cyclic positions
\(\mathbb Z/b\mathbb Z\), and let \(L_j\) be the total load in the cyclic
interval \([j,j+r)\).  Then
\[
        \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i v_i<p\right]
        =\frac1b\,\mathbb E\,\#\{j:L_j<r/b\}.              \tag{8}
\]
Consequently the rational case of Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent
to the averaged cyclic inequality
\[
        \mathbb E\,\#\{j:L_j<r/b\}\le b-r .                \tag{9}
\]
The corresponding deterministic assertion for each fixed load vector is false.
For \(r/b=2/7\), the cyclic load vector
\[
   \left(\frac14,\frac14,0,\frac14,0,\frac14,0\right)
\]
has six of its seven adjacent 2-intervals of load \(1/4<2/7\), and only the
interval through the first two positions is heavy.  Thus a cyclic proof would
have to use the randomness of the allocation, not just a pointwise interval
count.

There is also a useful sequential identity for \(0<p\le1/3\).  Fix any ordering
of the original weights and put \(S_i=\sum_{j\le i}w_jv_j\), \(S_0=0\).  Since
the increments are non-negative,
\[
 \Pr[S_i<p]=\Pr[S_{i-1}<p]
      -p\,\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p].
\]
Iterating gives
\[
 \Pr[S_m<p]
   =1-p\sum_{i=1}^m\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p].              \tag{10}
\]
Hence the small-\(p\) assertion is equivalent to the crossing-intensity bound
\[
        \sum_{i=1}^m\Pr[p-w_i\le S_{i-1}<p]\ge1
\]
for every deterministic ordering of the weights.

Here are exact reformulations of Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.

First, after replacing \(v_i\) by \(1-v_i\), it is equivalent to
\[
   q\ge\frac23,\quad \eta_i\stackrel{\rm iid}{\sim}\Ber(q)
   \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right]\le q ,
   \qquad q=1-p .                                      \tag{1}
\]
The inequality is strict on the left because
\(\sum_i w_i v_i\ge p\) is the same as
\(\sum_iw_i(1-v_i)\le1-p\).

Second, it is equivalent to the following two-tail pivot inequality: for every
\(0\le a\le p\), every set of weights \(u_i\ge0\) with
\(\sum_i u_i=1-a\), and i.i.d.\ \(v_i\sim\Ber(p)\),
\[
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i\ge p\right]
   \ge
   \frac{p}{1-p}\,
   \Pr\!\left[\sum_i u_i v_i<p-a\right].                 \tag{2}
\]
Indeed, (2) with \(a=0\) gives
\(U\ge\frac{p}{1-p}(1-U)\), hence \(U\ge p\).  Conversely, if
Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is known, add one extra weight \(a\).  Conditioning on
its Bernoulli variable gives
\[
 p\,\Pr[Y\ge p-a]+(1-p)\Pr[Y\ge p]\ge p,
\]
where \(Y=\sum_i u_i v_i\); rearranging gives (2).

Third, let \(\mu_p\) denote \(p\)-biased product measure on \(2^{[m]}\).  The
small-\(p\) assertion is equivalent to the following convex-hull statement: for
every non-empty down-set \(\mathcal D\subseteq 2^{[m]}\),
\[
       \mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p
       \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
       (p,\ldots,p)\in
       \conv\{\mathbf 1_A:\,A\in\mathcal D\}.             \tag{3}
\]
For the forward implication, take
\(\mathcal D=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\).  If (3) supplied a distribution on
\(\mathcal D\) with all coordinate marginals \(p\), then
\[
 p=\sum_i p w_i
   =\mathbb E\sum_{i\in A}w_i
   <p,
\]
a contradiction.

Conversely, suppose (3) fails for a down-set \(\mathcal D\), and put
\(P(\mathcal D)=\conv\{\mathbf1_A:A\in\mathcal D\}\).  This convex set is
coordinatewise down-closed: if \(x\in P(\mathcal D)\) and \(0\le y\le x\),
represent \(x\) as the marginals of a random \(A\in\mathcal D\), then thin each
\(i\in A\) independently with probability \(y_i/x_i\) (with the obvious
convention when \(x_i=0\)).  The resulting random subset is still in
\(\mathcal D\) and has marginals \(y\).  Thus \(P(\mathcal D)\) is disjoint
from the closed upper orthant
\((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\).  Since \(P(\mathcal D)\) is compact and
the two closed convex sets have positive distance, strict separation gives a
non-zero normal vector \(c\).  The vector must have \(c_i\ge0\) for
each \(i\): otherwise the linear functional \(x\mapsto c\cdot x\) would be
unbounded below on the upper orthant
\((p,\ldots,p)+\mathbb R_{\ge0}^m\) and could not separate it from the compact
polytope \(P(\mathcal D)\).  Thus
\[
      \sum_{i\in A}c_i < p\sum_i c_i
      \qquad(A\in\mathcal D)
\]
for some non-zero \(c\ge0\).
After normalizing \(w_i=c_i/\sum_jc_j\), Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} would imply
\[
 \mu_p(\mathcal D)
 \le \Pr\!\left[\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\right]\le1-p,
\]
contradicting \(\mu_p(\mathcal D)>1-p\).

Equivalently, with \(\lambda=p/(1-p)\), the remaining down-set statement is
the partition-function bound
\[
 p{\bf 1}\notin P(\mathcal D)
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 \sum_{A\in\mathcal D}\lambda^{|A|}
 \le (1+\lambda)^{m-1},
 \qquad 0\le\lambda\le\frac12 .                         \tag{4}
\]

The following slice formulation records the sharpest induction target I have
isolated.  If
\[
 \mathcal D=\mathcal E\cup\{A\cup\{n+1\}:A\in\mathcal F\},\qquad
 \mathcal F\subseteq\mathcal E\subseteq2^{[n]},
\]
then \(Z_\lambda(\mathcal D)=Z_\lambda(\mathcal E)+\lambda Z_\lambda(\mathcal F)\).
For \(c\ge0\), put
\[
 r_{\mathcal F}(c)=\max_{A\in\mathcal F}c(A),\qquad
 \rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)=
 \max\{c\cdot x:x\in P(\mathcal E),\ 0\le x_i\le\lambda\}.
\]
The condition \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal D)\) separates the down-convex set
\(P(\mathcal F)\) from the reflected capped set
\(\{y:\lambda({\bf1}-y)\in P(\mathcal E)\}\).  Thus the missing induction would
follow from the following capped replacement lemma:
\[
 \rho^\lambda_{\mathcal E}(c)+\lambda r_{\mathcal F}(c)<\lambda c([n])
 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad
 Z_\lambda(\mathcal E)+\lambda Z_\lambda(\mathcal F)\le(1+\lambda)^n .
\]
This lemma is not proved here; it is included to make the remaining obstruction
precise.  The uncapped scalar diagonal reach loses information in exactly the
mixed-slice case \(p{\bf1}\in P(\mathcal E)\) but \(p{\bf1}\notin P(\mathcal F)\).

Finally, for \(0<p\le1/3\), the same down-set has a useful
intersection-theoretic description.  If
\(\mathcal D_w=\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\) and
\(r=\lfloor1/p\rfloor\), then no \(r\) members of \(\mathcal D_w\) cover
\([m]\), because the weight of their union is \(<rp\le1\).  Equivalently, the
complement family
\[
   \mathcal H_w=\{B:\sum_{i\in B}w_i>1-p\}
\]
is \(r\)-wise intersecting.  Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is therefore the special
weighted-threshold assertion that such a family has \((1-p)\)-biased measure at
most \(1-p\).

\section*{A coalescence identity and a failed route}

The complement form also gives a useful local identity.  Put \(q=1-p\) and,
for a probability vector \(w\), define
\[
      \Phi_q(w)=\Pr\!\left[\sum_i w_i\eta_i>q\right],
      \qquad \eta_i\stackrel{\rm iid}{\sim}\Ber(q).
\]
Thus Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing} is equivalent to \(\Phi_q(w)\le q\) for all
\(q\ge2/3\).

If two coordinates of weights \(x\le y\) are replaced by one coordinate of
weight \(x+y\), and \(Z\) denotes the contribution of all other coordinates,
then direct conditioning on \(Z\) gives
\begin{equation}
\begin{aligned}
&\Phi_q(\ldots,x+y,\ldots)-\Phi_q(\ldots,x,y,\ldots)        \\
&\qquad
 =pq\Big(
       \Pr[q-x-y<Z\le q-y]
       -\Pr[q-x<Z\le q]
     \Big),
\end{aligned}                                      \tag{5}
\end{equation}
with the displayed half-open endpoints corresponding to the strict event
\(\sum w_i\eta_i>q\).

A tempting induction would be to prove that, unless only one coordinate remains,
some pair has non-negative gain in (5).  This is false, even in the capped regime
\(w_i<p\).  For example, take
\[
        p=\frac3{10},\qquad q=\frac7{10},\qquad
        w_1=w_2=w_3=w_4=\frac14 .
\]
Then
\[
   \Phi_q(w)=\Pr[{\rm Bin}(4,q)\ge3]
            =4q^3(1-q)+q^4=\frac{6517}{10000}.
\]
After coalescing any pair the vector is \((1/2,1/4,1/4)\), and the event
\[
        \frac12\eta_0+\frac14\eta_1+\frac14\eta_2>0.7
\]
occurs exactly when the merged coordinate succeeds and at least one of the two
remaining coordinates succeeds.  Hence the new value is
\[
        q\bigl(1-(1-q)^2\bigr)=\frac{637}{1000},
\]
which is smaller.  Thus pairwise coalescence cannot by itself prove
Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.

The obstruction is not isolated.  If \(n\ge4\),
\(1/n<p<1/(n-1)\), and \(w_i=1/n\), then \(w_i<p\) and
\[
 \Phi_q(w)=q^n+npq^{n-1},
\]
because the \(p\)-biased failure set has weight \(<p\) exactly when it is empty
or a singleton.  Coalescing any pair gives
\[
 \Phi_q(w^{ij})=q^{n-1}+(n-2)pq^{n-2},
\]
so
\[
 \Phi_q(w^{ij})-\Phi_q(w)
   =p q^{\,n-2}\bigl(n-2-(n-1)q\bigr)<0 .
\]
Any successful coalescence proof must therefore use larger or adaptive blocks,
or combine coalescence with a separate treatment of these reciprocal-chamber
configurations.
\section*{Remaining open issues}

The negative direction is complete, and the positive direction is complete for
the values listed in Corollary~\ref{cor:positive}, their finite products under
Proposition~\ref{prop:closure}, and \(p=1/2,1\).  Therefore a full solution
would follow from Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}; combined with
Proposition~\ref{prop:negative}, it would give exactly
\([0,1/3]\cup\{1/2,1\}\).

The essential missing assertion is Conjecture~\ref{conj:missing}.  Mathematically, it
is the assertion that every weighted threshold down-set
\(\{A:\sum_{i\in A}w_i<p\}\) has \(p\)-biased measure at most \(1-p\) when
\(p\le1/3\).  It appears in the document as the unproved implication
\([0,1/3]\subseteq\mathcal G\).  The approaches recorded above reduce it to
any one of the unit-gap inequality (UG), the strict complement inequality (1),
the pivot inequality (2), the convex-hull statement (3), or the
partition-function inequality (4).  Proposition~\ref{prop:closed} shows that it
would also be enough to prove the inequality on any dense subset of
\([0,1/3]\), or on the base interval \([1/6,1/3]\) by
Corollary~\ref{cor:base}.

The pairwise coalescence assertion suggested by formula (5) is not available:
the equal-weight examples in the preceding section show that every pair can have
negative coalescence gain.  A viable coalescence argument would need either block
coalescence or a dichotomy proving that configurations with all pair gains
negative already obey \(\Phi_q(w)\le q\).

The capped slice lemma displayed after (4) is the most focused remaining
inductive target.  Exhaustive enumeration of all down-sets on at most five
coordinates found no violation of the convex-hull statement (3), and the
extremal separated examples were only the expected dictatorship equalities and
lower-measure singleton-cone examples.  These computations are evidence for
(3), but of course not a proof.

The complement form is also adjacent to the hypotenuse case \(t=q\) of the
``bold play'' problem studied by Fokkink--Meester--Pelekis~\cite{FMP}: maximize
\(\Pr(\sum c_i\beta_i\ge t)\) over convex combinations of iid
\(\Ber(q)\) variables.  For a fixed finite weight vector the strict event
\(\sum c_i\beta_i>q\) is the same as \(\sum c_i\beta_i\ge t_*\), where
\(t_*\) is the next attainable subset sum above \(q\).  Their published
results prove several adjacent rectangles and reciprocal boundary points, but
the near-hypotenuse triangles with \(q<t_*\) close to \(q\), needed for all
\(q\ge2/3\), are not derived here from those results.  Frankl-type
intersection theorems~\cite{Frankl} explain some of the surrounding extremal
combinatorics, but they do not presently supply the weighted-threshold biased
measure bound (4).

\begin{center}
{\bf Partial proof: the first alternative and the remaining hard estimate}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I write the small universal constant in the problem as \(\kappa\), reserving
\(k\) for \(k\)-dilation.  Rectangles are oriented Euclidean products and
degree \(1\) means relative degree \(1\) on
\(H_4(R,\partial R;\mathbb Z)\to H_4(S,\partial S;\mathbb Z)\).

The current proof is complete through the first alternative and through a
conditional reduction of the hard case.  It is not a complete proof of the
stated dichotomy, because the averaged tightening estimate \((\mathrm{EAT})\)
below remains unproved.  The arguments are written for smooth transverse maps;
piecewise smooth maps may be treated by the same current-slicing argument, or
by smoothing on strata with an arbitrarily small \(C^1\)-loss and then
absorbing that loss into the universal constants.

\section*{Standard inputs}

All constants are positive and universal.  If \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\), then
\(\Dil_3(f),\Dil_4(f)\le1\): for singular values
\(\sigma_1\ge\cdots\ge\sigma_4\), the inequality
\(\sigma_1\sigma_2\le1\) implies \(\sigma_2\le1\) and
\(\sigma_1\cdots\sigma_j\le\sigma_1\sigma_2\) for \(j\ge2\).

We use Guth's map-of-pairs estimate \cite[Estimate 1]{GuthExpanding}.  For
\(n=4,k=2,j=1,l=4\) it gives
\begin{equation}\label{guth14}
        R_1^2\,\Vol(R)\ge c_0\,S_1^3S_2S_3S_4 .
\end{equation}
We also use the small-volume relative isoperimetric estimate in rectangles
\cite[Theorem 3]{GuthExpanding}: if \(Z\) is a relative \(2\)-cycle in \(R\)
with \(A=\Mass Z\le c_*R_1R_2\), then
\[
        \Fill_R(Z)\le C_*\max\{A^{3/2},A^2/R_1\}.
\]
Consequently
\begin{equation}\label{profile-inverse}
\Fill_R(Z)\ge L\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Mass Z\ge c\min\{R_1R_2,L^{2/3},(R_1L)^{1/2}\},
\end{equation}
and, after decreasing \(c_*\),
\begin{equation}\label{iso}
        \Mass Z<c_*R_1R_2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
        \Fill_R(Z)\le C R_1R_2^2 .
\end{equation}

\section*{The first alternative}

Let \(Q\) be the concentric subrectangle of the \((x_3,x_4)\)-face of \(S\)
with side lengths \(S_3/2,S_4/2\), and set
\[
        P_y=[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]\times\{y\},\qquad y\in Q.
\]

\begin{lemma}[central target plane]\label{central}
Every relative \(3\)-chain in \(S\) with boundary \(P_y\), \(y\in Q\), has
mass at least \(cS_1S_2S_3\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Choose a \(1\)-Lipschitz function \(\psi\) on the
\((x_3,x_4)\)-rectangle which vanishes on its boundary and satisfies
\(\psi\ge cS_3\) on \(Q\).  Put \(\beta=\psi\,dx_1\wedge dx_2\).  Then
\(d\beta\) has comass at most \(1\), and Stokes' theorem gives
\[
\Mass Y\ge\left|\int_Yd\beta\right|
 =\left|\int_{P_y}\beta\right|\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\]
The relative boundary terms vanish because \(dx_1\wedge dx_2=0\) on the
\(x_1,x_2\)-faces and \(\psi=0\) on the \(x_3,x_4\)-faces.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{firstalt}
There is \(c_2>0\) such that, under the hypotheses of the problem, if
\[
        R_1R_2^2\le c_2S_1S_2S_3 ,
\]
then \(R_3R_4\ge c_2S_3S_4\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(T=[[R]]\) be the fundamental relative current and put \(F=(f_3,f_4)\).
Since \(f\) has relative degree \(1\), \(f_\#T=[[S]]\) as a relative
\(4\)-current.  For a.e. \(y\in Q\), slicing naturality gives
\[
        f_\#\langle T,F,y\rangle
        =\langle [[S]],(x_3,x_4),y\rangle=\pm P_y .
\]
Write \(Z_y=\langle T,F,y\rangle\).  Any relative filling of \(Z_y\) in \(R\)
pushes forward, using \(\Dil_3(f)\le1\), to a filling of \(P_y\) in \(S\).
Lemma \ref{central} therefore gives
\[
        \Fill_R(Z_y)\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\]
If \(c_2\) is sufficiently small, \eqref{iso} forces
\(\Mass Z_y\ge c_*R_1R_2\).  Since \(J_2F\le\Dil_2(f)\le1\), the coarea
formula yields
\[
 \Vol(R)\ge\int_Q\Mass Z_y\,dy
        \ge cR_1R_2S_3S_4 .
\]
Cancelling \(R_1R_2\) proves the proposition.
\end{proof}

\section*{The unconditional partial hard-case bound}

Assume \(R_1=\alpha S_1\le S_1\) and that the first conclusion fails with a
small constant.  Proposition \ref{firstalt} gives
\begin{equation}\label{r12large}
        R_1R_2^2\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\end{equation}
For the slices above, \eqref{profile-inverse} with
\(L=cS_1S_2S_3\) gives
\[
\Mass Z_y\ge c\min\{R_1R_2,(S_1S_2S_3)^{2/3},
                         (R_1S_1S_2S_3)^{1/2}\}.
\]
Using \eqref{r12large} and \(R_1=\alpha S_1\), every term in the minimum is
at least \(c\alpha^{1/2}S_1(S_2S_3)^{1/2}\).  Hence
\begin{equation}\label{slicevol}
        \Vol(R)\ge
        c\alpha^{1/2}S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4 .
\end{equation}
On the other hand, \eqref{guth14} gives
\begin{equation}\label{monovol}
        \Vol(R)\ge
        c\alpha^{-2}S_1S_2S_3S_4 .
\end{equation}
Optimizing between \eqref{slicevol} and \eqref{monovol} gives
\[
        \Vol(R)\ge cS_1S_2^{3/5}S_3^{7/5}S_4 .
\]
Thus the remaining gap is exactly the high-aspect factor
\((S_3/S_2)^{1/10}\).

\section*{A sufficient averaged hard-case estimate}

Let \(G=(f_1,f_2)\), keep \(F=(f_3,f_4)\), and define
\[
E_y=\int_{Z_y}\|dG|_{TZ_y}\|_{HS}^2\,d\|Z_y\|,\qquad
I=\int_QE_y\,dy,\qquad q=|Q|.
\]
At a regular point of \(F\), let \(K=\ker dF\), and let \(a\ge b\) be the
singular values of \(dF|_{K^\perp}\); then \(ab=J_2F\).  For any unit
\(w\in K\), the mixed two-vector \(df(w)\wedge df(v)\), where \(v\) is a
unit vector with \(|dF(v)|=a\), has a component of norm
\(|dG(w)|a\).  Since \(\Dil_2(f)\le1\), \(|dG(w)|a\le1\), and hence
\(|dG(w)|^2J_2F\le b/a\le1\).  Summing over an orthonormal basis of \(K\)
gives
\[
        \|dG|_{\ker dF}\|_{HS}^2J_2F\le2 .
\]
By coarea,
\begin{equation}\label{Icoarea}
        I\le C\Vol(R).
\end{equation}
Let \(\Omega=\{y\in Q:E_y\le4I/q\}\), so \(|\Omega|\ge3q/4\).

For real normal currents define
\[
\EFill_f(y)=\inf\{\Mass C:\ C\in N_3(R,\partial R;\mathbb R),\
        f_\#(\partial C)=\pm P_y\}.
\]
This class is nonempty by filling the actual slice \(Z_y\).  If \(C\) is
admissible, then \(f_\#C\) fills \(P_y\) and \(\Mass(f_\#C)\le\Mass C\);
Lemma \ref{central} gives
\begin{equation}\label{EFilllower}
        \EFill_f(y)\ge cS_1S_2S_3 .
\end{equation}
Therefore
\begin{equation}\label{AElower}
A_\Omega:=\int_\Omega\EFill_f(y)\,dy
        \ge cS_1S_2S_3\,q
        \ge cS_1S_2S_3^2S_4 .
\end{equation}

For \(z\in Q\), let \({\mathcal B}_z\) be the set of \(x\in R\) such that
\(z\) belongs to the \(F\)-image of at least one coordinate \(2\)-plaque
through \(x\).  For fixed \(x\), the set of such \(z\)'s has measure at most
\(CR_3R_4\), because there are six plaques, each has area at most \(R_3R_4\),
and \(J_2F\le1\) on each plaque.  Hence, for every normal \(3\)-current \(B\),
\begin{equation}\label{badfubini}
 \frac1q\int_Q\Mass(B\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dz
 \le C\frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}\Mass B .
\end{equation}

The following estimate is sufficient for the desired theorem.  Take measurable
near-minimizers \(C_y^0\) with
\(\Mass C_y^0\le2\EFill_f(y)\); standard measurable selection in the
separable flat topology, or equivalently outer integration with
\(\varepsilon\)-minimizers, is enough for this formal step.  One needs
\begin{equation}\tag{EAT}\label{EAT}
 A_\Omega
 \le C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
 +\frac{C}{q}\int_Q\int_\Omega
       \Mass(C_y^0\llcorner{\mathcal B}_z)\,dy\,dz .
\end{equation}

Assuming \eqref{EAT}, \eqref{badfubini} and
\(\Mass C_y^0\le2\EFill_f(y)\) give
\[
A_\Omega\le C\left(R_1I+\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\right)
  +C\frac{R_3R_4}{S_3S_4}A_\Omega .
\]
If \(R_3R_4\le\kappa S_3S_4\), the last term is absorbed by taking
\(\kappa\) small.  If also \(R_1\le\kappa S_1\) and
\(I\le\delta S_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4\), then, since \(q\sim S_3S_4\),
\[
R_1I\le C\kappa\delta S_1S_2S_3^2S_4,\qquad
\frac{I^2}{S_1q}\le C\delta^2S_1S_2S_3^2S_4 .
\]
For small \(\delta,\kappa\) this contradicts \eqref{AElower}.  Thus
\(I\ge cS_1S_2^{1/2}S_3^{3/2}S_4\), and \eqref{Icoarea} gives the desired
second alternative.

\section*{Why the remaining estimate is non-routine}

The estimate \eqref{EAT} cannot be obtained by a standard source-local
cellular deformation in the quotient by \(f\)-null chains without a new idea.
The obstruction is already visible in a two-cell model.  Let \(A,A'\) be two
oriented \(2\)-cells with \(f_\#A=f_\#A'=P\).  Then \(N=A-A'\in\ker f_\#\).
If an exact-plaque bad set contains \(A\) but not \(A'\), the source-local
projection \(P_{\rm bad}\) with \(P_{\rm bad}A=A\), \(P_{\rm bad}A'=0\) gives
\(f_\#P_{\rm bad}N=P\ne0\).  Dually, a quotient cochain has equal values on
\(A\) and \(A'\), but restricting it to the nonsaturated bad set destroys that
property.  Thus exact source localization and quotient invariance are in
tension.

This does not disprove \eqref{EAT}; it identifies what is missing.  A proof
would need an energy-sensitive, null-respecting carrier or a dual target-side
cochain homotopy whose residual term is nevertheless controlled by the
unsaturated exact-plaque mass in \eqref{badfubini}.

\section*{Remaining open issues}

The document is therefore a conditional reduction, not a complete solution.

\begin{enumerate}
\item The essential mathematical gap is \eqref{EAT}, an averaged tightening
estimate for the degree-carrying part of the slices \(Z_y\).  It is used only
in the last section, but it is indispensable for the high-aspect hard case.

\item The first alternative, the single-slice bound, the weighted coarea
estimate, the essential filling lower bound, the exact-plaque Fubini estimate,
and the absorption algebra are proved above.

\item Standard approaches tried so far do not close the gap: monomial
degree estimates give only \eqref{guth14}; ordinary source deformations create
uncontrolled \(f\)-null boundary errors; quotient cochains are not compatible
with nonsaturated exact-plaque localization as shown above.

\item A complete proof would require either a proof of \eqref{EAT}, an
equivalent dual formulation using only target-pullback cochains, or a different
hard-regime argument exploiting the full two-parameter family of slices and the
ambient mixed \(2\)-Jacobian bounds.
\end{enumerate}

\begin{center}
{\bf Uniqueness of the invariant measure}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Put \(E=C_0([0,1])\), \(H=L^2(0,1)\), and let \(S_t\) be the Dirichlet heat
semigroup generated by \(\frac12\Delta\).  I use the Dirichlet version of the
Athreya--Butkovsky--L\^e--Mytnik approximation assumption in the following
form.  For each deterministic initial condition \(x\in E\), if \(b_n=G_{1/n}\),
then the regularized equation has the mild solution
\[
  u_t^n=S_t x+V_t+K_t^n,\qquad
  K_t^n=\int_0^tS_{t-r}b_n(u_r^n)\,dr,\qquad
  V_t=\int_0^tS_{t-r}\,dW_r ,
\]
and, for every \(t>0\) and every \(\Phi\in C_b(E)\),
\[
        P_t^n\Phi(x):=\E\Phi(u_t^n)\longrightarrow P_t\Phi(x).
        \tag{1}
\]
Thus I interpret the stated weak convergence of \(u^n(t)\) to \(u(t)\) as
holding for all deterministic starting points; this is the form needed for a
statement about invariant measures of the whole Markov semigroup.

\begin{lemma}[Uniform estimate on the regularized drift]\label{lem:Hestimate}
For each \(T<\infty\) there is \(C_T<\infty\), independent of \(n\) and
\(x\in E\), such that
\[
 \|K_t^n-S_{t-s}K_s^n\|_{L^2(\Omega;H)}
      \le C_T |t-s|^{3/4},\qquad 0\le s<t\le T .             \tag{2}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_t\) be the Dirichlet kernel and \(d(y)=y\wedge(1-y)\).  For
\(0<a\le 2T\), the reflection formula for the interval and comparison with
the two half-lines at the nearer boundary give
\[
p_a(z,y)\le Ca^{-1/2}e^{-|z-y|^2/(Ca)},\qquad
p_a(y,y)\asymp a^{-1/2}\bigl(1\wedge d(y)^2/a\bigr).          \tag{3}
\]
For example, if \(d(y)\le\sqrt a\), the half-line expression
\((2\pi a)^{-1/2}(1-e^{-2d(y)^2/a})\) is comparable to \(d(y)^2a^{-3/2}\);
if \(d(y)\ge\sqrt a\), the reflected terms are exponentially smaller than
the free diagonal term.  The finitely many remaining images are absorbed in a
constant depending only on \(T\).  Hence, for
\(\rho_\tau(y)=\int_0^\tau p_{2r}(y,y)\,dr\),
\[
        \rho_\tau(y)\asymp \sqrt{\tau}\wedge d(y),\qquad
        \int_0^1\rho_\tau(y)^{-1/2}dy\le C_T\tau^{-1/4}.      \tag{4}
\]
Indeed the integral of \(r^{-1/2}(1\wedge d^2/r)\) is comparable to
\(\sqrt\tau\) when \(d\ge\sqrt\tau\), and to \(d\) otherwise.  Also
\[
 \sup_z\int_0^1p_a(z,y)\rho_\tau(y)^{-1/2}dy
 \le C_T(a^{-1/4}+\tau^{-1/4}).                              \tag{5}
\]
To see this, use \(\rho_\tau^{-1/2}\le C(\tau^{-1/4}+d^{-1/2})\).  The first
part integrates to \(C\tau^{-1/4}\).  For the second, bound
\(d(y)^{-1/2}\le y^{-1/2}+(1-y)^{-1/2}\) and use
\(y=\sqrt a\,r\) near the left boundary (similarly near the right) to get
\(Ca^{-1/4}\sup_{\alpha\ge0}\int_0^\infty
e^{-(r-\alpha)^2/C}r^{-1/2}dr<\infty\).  Finally, for \(0<\tau\le T\),
\[
        \|\rho_\tau^{-1}S_\tau f\|_2\le C_T\tau^{-1/2}\|f\|_2. \tag{6}
\]
Here \(\rho_\tau^{-1}\le C(\tau^{-1/2}+d^{-1})\); \(S_\tau f\in H^1_0\);
Hardy's inequality gives \(\|S_\tau f/d\|_2\le C\|\partial_xS_\tau f\|_2\);
and \(\|\partial_xS_\tau f\|_2\le C\tau^{-1/2}\|f\|_2\).

Write \(D^n_{s,t}=K_t^n-S_{t-s}K_s^n\).  Freeze the equation at time \(s\):
\[
 A^n_{s,t}=\int_s^tS_{t-r}
 b_n\!\left(S_{r-s}u_s^n+V_r-S_{r-s}V_s\right)\,dr .          \tag{7}
\]
For fixed \(n\), if \(\Pi\) is a partition of \([s,t]\), then
\[
 \sum_{[a,b]\in\Pi}S_{t-b}A^n_{a,b}\to D^n_{s,t}\quad\hbox{in }L^2(H).
 \tag{8}
\]
Indeed the summand differs from
\(\int_a^bS_{b-r}b_n(u_r^n)\,dr\) by at most
\(C_n(b-a)^2\) in \(L^2(H)\), because \(b_n\) is Lipschitz and the two
arguments differ only by the drift accumulated on \([a,r]\), whose \(H\)-norm
is at most \(C_n(r-a)\).  After applying \(S_{t-b}\) and summing, the error is
\(O_n((t-s)|\Pi|)\).

The estimates below are uniform in \(n\).  Given \(\calF_s\),
\[
Z_r(y):=S_{r-s}u_s^n(y)+V_r(y)-S_{r-s}V_s(y)
\]
is Gaussian with variance \(\rho_{r-s}(y)\).  Since \(b_n\) is a probability
density, \(\sup_m\E b_n(m+N(0,\sigma^2))\le C\sigma^{-1}\).  Moreover, given
\(\calF_r\), the future part of \(Z_q(z)\), \(q>r\), has variance
\[
 \int_r^q\!\!\int_0^1p_{q-\ell}(z,\xi)^2\,d\xi d\ell
 =\int_0^{q-r}p_{2a}(z,z)\,da=\rho_{q-r}(z).
\]
Thus, for \(s<r<q\),
\[
 \E_s\,b_n(Z_r(y))b_n(Z_q(z))
 \le C\rho_{r-s}(y)^{-1/2}\rho_{q-r}(z)^{-1/2}.              \tag{9}
\]
Using
\(\int p_{t-r}(x,y)p_{t-q}(x,z)dx=p_{2t-r-q}(y,z)\), then (5) in \(y\) and
(4) in \(z\), we obtain
\[
 \E_s\|A^n_{s,t}\|_2^2
 \le C\!\int_s^t\!\!\int_r^t
      \bigl((2t-r-q)^{-1/4}+(r-s)^{-1/4}\bigr)(q-r)^{-1/4}\,dq\,dr
 \le C|t-s|^{3/2}.                                          \tag{10}
\]
The last inequality is just scaling on the triangle
\(0\le r-s\le q-s\le t-s\).  Hence
\(\|A^n_{s,t}\|_{L^2H}\le C|t-s|^{3/4}\).

For \(s<u<t\), let
\(\delta A^n_{s,u,t}=A^n_{s,t}-S_{t-u}A^n_{s,u}-A^n_{u,t}\).  The pieces before
\(u\) cancel.  For \(r>u\), the two frozen arguments differ by
\(S_{r-u}D^n_{s,u}\).  Conditional on \(\calF_u\), the remaining noise at
space point \(y\) has variance \(\rho_{r-u}(y)\), and
\(\|(b_n*\varphi_\sigma)'\|_\infty\le C\sigma^{-2}\), uniformly in \(n\).
Therefore
\[
 \left|\E_u\{b_n(Z_r(y))-b_n(Z_r(y)+h(y))\}\right|
      \le C\rho_{r-u}(y)^{-1}|h(y)|.
\]
With \(h=S_{r-u}D^n_{s,u}\), (6) and contraction of \(S_t\) imply
\[
        \|\E_u\delta A^n_{s,u,t}\|_2
        \le C(t-u)^{1/2}\|D^n_{s,u}\|_2 .                    \tag{11}
\]
Taking \(\E_s\) and using Jensen gives the same estimate with \(\E_s\) in
place of \(\E_u\).

We need only the following dyadic sewing fact.  Suppose \(A_{s,t}\) is
\(H\)-valued and \(\calF_t\)-measurable, and for every dyadic interval
\([a,b]\) with midpoint \(u\),
\[
\|\E_a\delta A_{a,u,b}\|_{L^2H}\le \Gamma_1(b-a)^{1+\varepsilon_1},\quad
\|\delta A_{a,u,b}-\E_a\delta A_{a,u,b}\|_{L^2H}
        \le \Gamma_2(b-a)^{1/2+\varepsilon_2}.
\]
If \(J_m=\sum_iS_{t-t_{i+1}}A_{t_i,t_{i+1}}\) over the \(2^m\) equal
subintervals of \([s,t]\), then \(J_m\) converges and
\[
 \|{\cal A}_{s,t}-A_{s,t}\|_{L^2H}
 \le C(\Gamma_1|t-s|^{1+\varepsilon_1}
       +\Gamma_2|t-s|^{1/2+\varepsilon_2}).                 \tag{12}
\]
Indeed, \(J_{m+1}-J_m\) is the sum of
\(-S_{t-b}\delta A_{a,u,b}\).  The conditional-mean parts are bounded by the
triangle inequality, giving a factor
\(2^m(|t-s|2^{-m})^{1+\varepsilon_1}\).  The centered parts are orthogonal:
for two level-\(m\) intervals with the first lying earlier, the earlier
summand is \(\calF_a\)-measurable for the later one, while the later summand
has conditional mean zero given \(\calF_a\).  Thus the square norm is the sum
of the square norms.  Summing the resulting geometric series proves (12).

For fixed \(n\), \(Y_n(h)=\sup_{0<t-s\le h}\|D^n_{s,t}\|_{L^2H}/|t-s|^{3/4}\)
is finite, because \(b_n\) is bounded.  On intervals of length at most \(h\),
(11) gives
\[
 \|\E_a\delta A^n_{a,u,b}\|_{L^2H}\le C Y_n(h)(b-a)^{5/4}.
\]
Also
\[
\|\delta A^n_{a,u,b}-\E_a\delta A^n_{a,u,b}\|_{L^2H}
 \le 2\|\delta A^n_{a,u,b}\|_{L^2H}
 \le C(b-a)^{3/4},
\]
because \(\delta A\) is a sum of three \(A\)-terms and (10) applies to each.
The dyadic sewing limit is \(D^n_{s,t}\) by (8).  Combining (10) and (12),
\[
 \|D^n_{s,t}\|_{L^2H}\le C|t-s|^{3/4}
        +C Y_n(h)|t-s|^{5/4}.
\]
Thus \(Y_n(h)\le C+Ch^{1/2}Y_n(h)\).  Choose \(h_0>0\), depending only on
\(T\), so that \(Ch_0^{1/2}\le1/2\); then \(Y_n(h_0)\le2C\), uniformly in
\(n\).  For longer intervals use the additivity
\(D^n_{s,t}=S_{t-r}D^n_{s,r}+D^n_{r,t}\) and split into pieces of length at
most \(h_0\).  This gives a linear bound \(C_T|t-s|\), which is bounded by
\(C_T|t-s|^{3/4}\) on \(0\le t-s\le T\).  The lemma follows.
\end{proof}

\section*{The reversible reference measure and its gap}

Let \(\gamma\) be Brownian bridge measure on \(E\), the centered Gaussian
measure with covariance \((-\Delta_D)^{-1}\).  Define
\[
  \Lambda(v)=\int_0^1\1_{\{v(r)>0\}}\,dr,\qquad
  \pi(dv)=Z^{-1}e^{2\Lambda(v)}\,\gamma(dv).                 \tag{13}
\]
For \(B_n'=b_n\), \(B_n(a)=\int_{-\infty}^ab_n(r)dr\), put
\[
   \pi_n(dv)=Z_n^{-1}\exp\left(2\int_0^1B_n(v(r))\,dr\right)\gamma(dv).
\]
Since \(0\le B_n\le1\), \(B_n(a)\to\1_{\{a>0\}}\) for \(a\ne0\), and a
Brownian bridge spends zero Lebesgue time at a fixed level,
\[
        \|\pi_n-\pi\|_{\TV}\to0 .                            \tag{14}
\]
All densities \(d\pi_n/d\gamma\) and \(d\pi/d\gamma\) are bounded above and
below by deterministic positive constants.

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:reggap}
There are constants \(C,c>0\), independent of \(n\), such that for every
bounded continuous \(f,g\) and every \(t\ge0\),
\[
 \int P_t^n f\,d\pi_n=\int f\,d\pi_n,\qquad
 \int gP_t^n f\,d\pi_n=\int fP_t^n g\,d\pi_n,                \tag{15}
\]
and
\[
        \Var_{\pi_n}(P_t^n f)\le e^{-ct}\Var_{\pi_n}(f).      \tag{16}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\Pi_m\) be projection onto the first \(m\) Dirichlet modes and set
\[
        U_{n,m}(v)=\int_0^1B_n(\Pi_m v(r))\,dr .
\]
Its \(H\)-gradient is \(\nabla_HU_{n,m}(v)=\Pi_m b_n(\Pi_m v)\).  Let
\(P_t^{n,m}\) be the semigroup of
\[
u_t^{n,m}=S_t x+V_t+\int_0^tS_{t-r}\Pi_m b_n(\Pi_m u_r^{n,m})\,dr .
\]
The first \(m\) Fourier coordinates solve the finite-dimensional Langevin
diffusion
\[
dX_j=-(\lambda_j/2)X_j\,dt+\partial_j U_{n,m}(X)\,dt+d\beta_j,
\qquad j\le m,
\]
and the tail is an independent OU process.  Hence, by finite-dimensional
integration by parts and the standard OU integration by parts in the tail,
\(P_t^{n,m}\) is reversible with respect to
\(\pi_{n,m}=Z_{n,m}^{-1}e^{2U_{n,m}}\gamma\), and its Dirichlet form is
\(\calE_{n,m}(F,F)=\frac12\int\|\nabla_HF\|_2^2\,d\pi_{n,m}\) on smooth
cylinder functions.

The measures \(\pi_{n,m}\) converge to \(\pi_n\) in total variation: \(\Pi_m
v\to v\) in \(H\), \(\gamma\)-a.s., and \(B_n\) is bounded and Lipschitz.  For
fixed \(n\), \(u_t^{n,m}\to u_t^n\) in probability in \(E\).  In \(H\), this
follows from Gronwall, since \(\Pi_m b_n(\Pi_m z)\) is uniformly Lipschitz in
\(z\) for fixed \(n\), and for each \(H\)-valued \(y\),
\(\Pi_m b_n(\Pi_m y)\to b_n(y)\) in \(H\), dominated by \(2\|b_n\|_\infty\).
For convergence in \(E\) at a fixed \(t>0\), split the drift convolution into
\([0,t-\varepsilon]\) and \([t-\varepsilon,t]\).  On the first part,
\(S_{t-r}:H\to H^\alpha_0\subset E\), \(\alpha>1/2\), is bounded.  On the
short terminal part,
\[
 \int_{t-\varepsilon}^t\|S_{t-r}F_r\|_\infty dr
 \le C\|b_n\|_\infty\int_0^\varepsilon a^{-1/4}da,
\]
for both \(F_r=\Pi_m b_n(\Pi_mu_r^{n,m})\) and \(F_r=b_n(u_r^n)\), because
\(\Pi_m\) is an \(L^2\)-contraction and \(S_a:H\to E\) has norm \(Ca^{-1/4}\).
This tends to zero with \(\varepsilon\), uniformly in \(m\).

Consequently the reversible identities pass to \(m=\infty\).  For instance,
if \(h_m=d\pi_{n,m}/d\gamma\), then \(h_m\to h_n=d\pi_n/d\gamma\) in
\(L^1(\gamma)\) and the \(h_m\)'s are uniformly bounded; together with
\(P_t^{n,m}f(x)\to P_t^nf(x)\) and bounded convergence this gives (15).

It remains to record the gap uniformly.  The Gaussian Poincare inequality for
\(\gamma\) in \(H\)-gradient form is
\[
        \Var_\gamma(F)\le \lambda_1^{-1}\int\|\nabla_HF\|_2^2\,d\gamma
\]
on smooth cylinder functions.  Since all densities
\(d\pi_{n,m}/d\gamma\) lie in a fixed interval \([a_0,a_0^{-1}]\), the
Holley--Stroock bounded perturbation argument gives
\[
        \Var_{\pi_{n,m}}(F)\le C\,\calE_{n,m}(F,F),
\]
with \(C\) independent of \(n,m\).  For a symmetric Markov semigroup this
Poincare inequality is equivalent to exponential \(L^2\)-decay, so
\[
 \Var_{\pi_{n,m}}(P_t^{n,m}f)\le e^{-ct}\Var_{\pi_{n,m}}(f).
\]
Letting \(m\to\infty\), using the convergence just proved and the uniformly
bounded densities, yields (16).
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:gap}
The measure \(\pi\) is \(P_t\)-invariant, \(P_t\) is symmetric on
\(L^2(\pi)\), and
\[
       \Var_\pi(P_t f)\le e^{-ct}\Var_\pi(f),
       \qquad f\in L^2(\pi),\ t\ge0 .                       \tag{17}
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For \(f,g\in C_b(E)\), (1), (14), (15), and the uniform boundedness of the
densities imply, by dominated convergence with respect to \(\gamma\),
\[
 \int P_tf\,d\pi=\lim_n\int P_t^nf\,d\pi_n=\int f\,d\pi,\qquad
 \int gP_tf\,d\pi=\int fP_tg\,d\pi .
\]
On the Polish space \(E\), these identities identify the measure
\(\int\pi(dx)P_t(x,\cdot)\) with \(\pi\), and the measure
\(\pi(dx)P_t(x,dy)\) with its transpose; hence invariance and detailed balance
hold for all bounded Borel functions.  Similarly, (16) passes to the limit for
bounded continuous \(f\).  Finally \(C_b(E)\) is dense in \(L^2(\pi)\), and an
invariant Markov operator is an \(L^2\)-contraction, so symmetry and (17)
extend to all \(L^2(\pi)\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Fixed-time absolute continuity}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:control}
For every deterministic \(x\in E\) and every \(t>0\),
\[
        P_t(x,\cdot)\ll \gamma ,
\]
and hence \(P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\pi\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(\nu_t^x=\Law(S_tx+V_t)\).  We first bound
\(\Ent(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\mid\nu_t^x)\) uniformly in \(n\).  Put
\(r_k=t(1-2^{-k})\), \(\Delta_k=r_{k+1}-r_k\), and
\(D_k^n=K^n_{r_{k+1}}-S_{\Delta_k}K^n_{r_k}\).  Define
\[
 F_s^n=\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{\1_{(r_{k+1},r_{k+2}]}(s)}{\Delta_{k+1}}\,
        S_{s-r_{k+1}}D_k^n .                                \tag{18}
\]
The process is predictable because \(D_k^n\) is \(\calF_{r_{k+1}}\)-measurable.
Lemma \ref{lem:Hestimate} and contraction of \(S_t\) give
\[
 \E\int_0^t\|F_s^n\|_2^2ds
 \le C_t\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{\Delta_k^{3/2}}{\Delta_{k+1}}<\infty, \tag{19}
\]
uniformly in \(n\).  For partial sums,
\[
 \int_0^tS_{t-s}F_s^{n,N}ds
 =\sum_{k=0}^NS_{t-r_{k+1}}D_k^n
 =S_{t-r_{N+1}}K^n_{r_{N+1}},
\]
where \(F^{n,N}\) denotes the sum in (18) up to \(k=N\).  Since
\(r_{N+1}\uparrow t\), the regularized mild solution gives \(K^n\) continuous
with values in \(E\), and \(S_a\to I\) strongly on \(E\),
\[
        \int_0^tS_{t-s}F_s^n\,ds=K_t^n                       \tag{20}
\]
in probability in \(E\) (and hence in \(H\)).  The state space assertion is
legitimate because, for every deterministic \(F\in L^2(0,t;H)\),
\[
 \sum_j\lambda_j^\alpha\Big|\int_0^te^{-\lambda_j(t-s)/2}F_j(s)ds\Big|^2
 \le \sum_j\lambda_j^{\alpha-1}\int_0^t|F_j(s)|^2ds
 \le C\|F\|_{L^2(0,t;H)}^2
\]
for \(0<\alpha<1\); choosing \(\alpha>1/2\) gives an element of
\(H^\alpha_0(0,1)\subset E\).

We use the following standard entropy form of Girsanov, and recall its proof.
If \(F\) is predictable, \(H\)-valued, and has bounded energy, then
\[
 X^F=S_tx+V_t+\int_0^tS_{t-s}F_sds
\]
satisfies
\[
        \Ent(\Law(X^F)\mid\nu_t^x)
        \le \frac12\,\E\int_0^t\|F_s\|_2^2ds .               \tag{21}
\]
For the first \(M\) Brownian coordinates this is finite-dimensional
Girsanov: under the tilted measure, the shifted coordinates
\(\beta_j+\int_0^\cdot F_j(s)ds\), \(j\le M\), are Brownian, and the entropy
cost is \(\frac12\E\int_0^t\sum_{j\le M}F_j(s)^2ds\).  Applying data
processing to the deterministic-kernel stochastic convolution gives the same
bound for the first \(M\) terminal Fourier coordinates.  Since the Borel
sigma-field of \(E\) is generated by these coordinates and relative entropy
is the supremum over increasing finite-coordinate sigma-fields, (21) follows.

Localize \(F^n\) by
\(\tau_R=\inf\{s:\int_0^s\|F_r^n\|_2^2dr>R\}\) and
\(\widehat F^{\,n,R}=F^n\1_{[0,\tau_R]}\).  Let
\[
 X_R=S_tx+V_t+\int_0^tS_{t-s}\widehat F^{\,n,R}_sds .
\]
Then (21) and (19) give
\[
 \Ent(\Law(X_R)\mid\nu_t^x)
 \le \frac12\,\E\int_0^t\|F_s^n\|_2^2ds .                   \tag{22}
\]
On \(\{\tau_R>t\}\), (20) gives \(X_R=u_t^n\), while
\(\mathbb P(\tau_R\le t)\le R^{-1}\E\int_0^t\|F_s^n\|_2^2ds\to0\).  Hence
\(X_R\to u_t^n\) in probability in \(E\).  Lower semicontinuity of relative
entropy on the Polish space \(E\), then (19) and (22), yield
\[
        \sup_n\Ent(P_t^n(x,\cdot)\mid\nu_t^x)<\infty .       \tag{23}
\]
By the assumed weak convergence (1) and lower semicontinuity again,
\(\Ent(P_t(x,\cdot)\mid\nu_t^x)<\infty\).  Thus
\(P_t(x,\cdot)\ll\nu_t^x\).

It remains only to compare Gaussian measures.  In the basis
\(e_j(r)=\sqrt2\sin(j\pi r)\), \(\lambda_j=j^2\pi^2\), the covariance of
\(\nu_t^x\) has eigenvalues
\[
        q_j(t)=\frac{1-e^{-\lambda_jt}}{\lambda_j},
\]
whereas \(\gamma\) has eigenvalues \(\lambda_j^{-1}\).  The ratios
\(1-e^{-\lambda_jt}\) are bounded away from zero and differ from \(1\) by a
square-summable sequence.  The Cameron--Martin spaces are therefore both
\(H^1_0(0,1)\), and \(S_tx\in H^1_0\).  Feldman--Hajek gives
\(\nu_t^x\sim\gamma\).
\end{proof}

\section*{Conclusion}

Let \(\eta\) be an invariant probability measure.  If \(\pi(A)=0\), then
Lemma \ref{lem:control} gives \(P_t(y,A)=0\) for every \(y\in E\), and hence
\[
        \eta(A)=\int_E P_t(y,A)\,\eta(dy)=0.
\]
Thus \(\eta\ll\pi\); write \(d\eta=h\,d\pi\).  Detailed balance gives the
\(L^1\)-duality
\[
        \int f\,P_t g\,d\pi=\int P_t f\,g\,d\pi
\]
for bounded Borel \(f\) and \(g\in L^1(\pi)\), by truncating \(g\).  Therefore
\[
 \int f h\,d\pi=\int P_tf\,d\eta
      =\int P_tf\,h\,d\pi=\int f\,P_t h\,d\pi ,
\]
so \(P_t h=h\) in \(L^1(\pi)\).  For \(a>0\), let \(h_a=h\wedge a\).  Since
\(r\mapsto r\wedge a\) is concave,
\[
        P_t h_a\le (P_t h)\wedge a=h_a .
\]
The two sides have the same \(\pi\)-integral; therefore \(P_t h_a=h_a\).
Now \(h_a\in L^2(\pi)\), and the spectral gap (17) implies
\(\Var_\pi(h_a)=0\).  Thus \(h_a\) is constant for every \(a\).  Letting
\(a\uparrow\infty\) and using \(\int h\,d\pi=1\), we obtain \(h=1\)
\(\pi\)-a.s.  Hence \(\eta=\pi\).

Therefore \(P_t\) has at most one invariant probability measure on
\(C_0([0,1])\).  In fact, if an invariant probability exists, it is the Gibbs
measure \(\pi\) in (13).
