\begin{document}

\begin{center}
\textbf{A counterexample}
\end{center}

The answer is no.  We give a counterexample in
\(G=\langle a,b,c\rangle\).  Write \(\ol x=x^{-1}\), and set
\[
        w=\ol a\,a\, b\,\ol b\,\ol a\,a\, c\,\ol c\,\ol a\,a .
\]
This word is freely trivial, by deleting the adjacent inverse pairs
\[
        \ol a a,\qquad b\ol b,\qquad \ol a a,\qquad c\ol c,\qquad
        \ol a a .
\]
It is quasi-reduced: the only adjacent inverse pairs are the five
displayed ones, and in each case the following letter, if there is one,
is not the first letter of that pair.

We shall use two elementary consequences of the face condition in the
definition.  Compactify the upper half-plane to a disk and put the
marked boundary points in cyclic order.  For a matching \(M\), let
\(\Gamma(M)\) be the graph obtained from \(M\) by making each crossing a
vertex; the original boundary endpoints are leaves.

\begin{lemma}
For every admissible matching \(M\), the graph \(\Gamma(M)\) is a
forest.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Suppose that \(\Gamma(M)\) contains a cycle.  Boundary vertices have
valence one, so every cycle is contained in the interior of the disk.
Choose one which is innermost: the disk bounded by it contains no other
cycle.  It in fact contains no edge of \(\Gamma(M)\).  Indeed, any
component of the graph lying in that disk either contains a cycle, which
would be smaller, or is a tree.  Such a tree cannot be wholly interior,
because it would have an interior leaf; and if it attaches to the chosen
cycle in two places, the path between the attachment points gives a
smaller cycle.  Thus it would have to attach in exactly one place, again
forcing an interior leaf.  This is impossible since all interior vertices
have valence four and all leaves of \(\Gamma(M)\) lie on the boundary of
the disk.

Thus one complementary region has this cycle as a boundary component.
The corresponding component of
\(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is closed and has no boundary endpoint.
This contradicts the defining requirement that every such component
have boundary consisting of two marked boundary points with inverse
labels.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
Suppose that a letter \(x\) occurs in the word exactly once and
\(x^{-1}\) occurs exactly once.  Then, in every admissible matching, the
two corresponding endpoints are joined by an uncrossed interval.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let the two endpoints be \(p\) and \(q\).  At \(p\), the two local sides
of the incident edge belong to two components of face boundary.  By the
label condition, both of these components must end at \(q\), since
\(q\) is the only endpoint with the inverse label.

By the preceding lemma the component of \(\Gamma(M)\) containing \(p\)
is a tree, and hence there is a unique graph path from \(p\) to \(q\).
If this path met a crossing, let \(c\) be the first crossing encountered
when travelling from \(p\).  At \(c\), the two local face-boundary sides
of the incoming edge leave along two different half-edges.  Removing
\(c\) from the tree separates those two half-edges into different
components, so at most one of the two resulting face-boundary arcs can
continue to \(q\).  This contradicts the previous paragraph.  Therefore
the path from \(p\) to \(q\) has no crossing, and is a single uncrossed
interval.
\end{proof}

In the word \(w\), the letters \(b,\ol b,c,\ol c\) each occur only in
the displayed adjacent inverse pairs.  Hence every admissible matching
contains the uncrossed intervals \((3,4)\) and \((7,8)\).  Since their
endpoints are adjacent on the boundary, these intervals cut off empty
boundary disks.  Deleting them and renumbering the remaining endpoints
\(1,2,5,6,9,10\) gives a cell-preserving isomorphism from \(F_w\) to the
analogous six-endpoint complex for
\[
        u=\ol a\,a\,\ol a\,a\,\ol a\,a .
\]
Here \(u\) is only an auxiliary labelled boundary word; the same matching
definition is being used after the forced intervals have been removed.

We now compute this six-endpoint complex.  For \(u\), odd endpoints have
label \(\ol a\) and even endpoints have label \(a\).  Write
\[
        ij|kl|mn
\]
for the pairing \(\{i,j\},\{k,l\},\{m,n\}\).  By Lemma 1, two intervals
cannot cross twice, since two crossings between them would make a bigon
cycle.  Also, all three pairs of intervals cannot cross, since the
three crossings would form a triangular cycle.  Hence the crossing
pattern is determined by the endpoint pairing: two pairs cross exactly
when their endpoints interleave around the boundary.  There is no
further isotopy datum here; a regular neighbourhood of the resulting
planar forest is unique once the cyclic order of its six boundary leaves
is fixed.

Conversely, take any pairing for which the interleaving graph is a
forest, and draw it with one transverse crossing for each interleaving
pair and no other crossings.  Then \(\Gamma(M)\) is a forest.  Every
component of \(M\cap\partial\overline R\) is therefore an arc joining
two boundary endpoints.  Push such an arc slightly into the adjacent
face.  The pushed-off arc is disjoint from the diagram and cuts the disk
into two pieces.  No interval can have its endpoints on different sides,
so each side contains an even number of marked endpoints.  Therefore
the two endpoints of the face-boundary arc have opposite parity.  For
the alternating word \(u\), opposite parity is exactly the inverse-label
condition.  Thus precisely the pairings with forest interleaving graph
are admissible.  Among the fifteen pairings of six points, the only
excluded one is the all-crossing pairing \(14|25|36\).

The resulting cells are the following.  The five vertices are
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
v_0=12|34|56, & v_1=12|36|45, & v_2=14|23|56,\\
v_3=16|23|45, & v_4=16|25|34. &
\end{array}
\]
The six edges are
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
e_{01}=12|35|46, & e_{02}=13|24|56, & e_{13}=13|26|45,\\
e_{23}=15|23|46, & e_{04}=15|26|34, & e_{34}=16|24|35,
\end{array}
\]
where the subscripts indicate the two endpoint vertices.  The three
squares are
\[
        Q_{12}=13|25|46,\qquad
        Q_{14}=14|26|35,\qquad
        Q_{24}=15|24|36,
\]
and their boundaries, obtained by the two local resolutions of each
crossing, are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial Q_{12}&=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{02},\\
\partial Q_{14}&=e_{01}\cup e_{13}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04},\\
\partial Q_{24}&=e_{02}\cup e_{23}\cup e_{34}\cup e_{04}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Thus the one-skeleton is the theta graph with poles \(v_0\) and \(v_3\)
and with three length-two paths through \(v_1,v_2,v_4\).  The square
\(Q_{12}\) fills the cycle formed by the paths through \(v_1\) and
\(v_2\).  The square \(Q_{14}\) fills the cycle formed by the paths
through \(v_1\) and \(v_4\).  These two squares are glued along the
common path through \(v_1\), so their union is a disk whose boundary is
the remaining cycle, the one through \(v_2\) and \(v_4\).  Finally
\(Q_{24}\) is glued along exactly this boundary.  Hence the whole
complex is a 2-sphere:
\[
        F_w \cong S^2 .
\]
In particular \(F_w\) is not contractible.

\begin{center}
{\bf A polar transform for the relative Dressian}
\end{center}

\section*{0. Conventions and two local facts}

Let $E$ be the ground set, $r=\rk M$, and $\mu_M$ the trivial rank $r$
valuated matroid supported on the bases of $M$.  I use the standard
model of the relative Dressian: a rank $k$ point is a projective
valuated matroid $\nu$ with $\nu\preceq\mu_M$, where $\preceq$ denotes
valuated quotient.  Brandt--Eur--Zhang \cite{BEZ} identify this order with
inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces,
\[
        \Trop(\theta)\subseteq\Trop(\nu)
        \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \theta\preceq\nu .
\]
For valuated matroids $p,q$ of ranks $a\le b$, $p\preceq q$ is
equivalent to the incidence-Plucker relations
\[
 \min_{i\in J\setminus I}\{p(I\cup i)+q(J\setminus i)\}
 \quad\hbox{attained at least twice}                         \tag{IP}
\]
for all $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$.  All-$\infty$
minima are regarded as attained at every displayed index.

\begin{criterion}[nested incidence suffices]\label{crit:local}
Let $p,q$ be valuated matroids of ranks $a<b$ whose ordinary supports
are in quotient order.  If {\rm (IP)} holds whenever $I\subseteq J$,
then {\rm (IP)} holds for all $I,J$.
\end{criterion}

\begin{proof}
For a rank $a$ valuated matroid $p$ and an $(a-1)$-set $I$, write
$C^*_p(I)_e=p(I\cup e)$, with value $\infty$ if $e\in I$.  We use the
standard cocircuit-generation theorem of Dress--Wenzel/Speyer \cite{DW,Speyer}: the
tropical linear space of a valuated matroid is the closed tropical convex
hull of its valuated cocircuits.

Fix arbitrary $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$, and put
$c_j=q(J\setminus j)$ for $j\in J$.  If all $c_j$ are $\infty$ there is
nothing to prove.  Otherwise $J$ contains a $q$-basis.  Since the
ordinary support of $p$ is a quotient of the ordinary support of $q$,
every $q$-basis spans the support matroid of $p$ (indeed,
$a-\rk_p(Q)\le b-\rk_q(Q)=0$ for a $q$-basis $Q$); hence the
restriction $p|J$ has rank $a$.

For every $S\subseteq J$ with $|S|=a-1$, the nested hypothesis with
upper set $J$ says precisely that the cocircuit $C^*_{p|J}(S)$ is
tropically orthogonal to $c=(c_j)_{j\in J}$.  The set of vectors
orthogonal to a fixed $c$ is a tropical hyperplane, hence is tropically
convex; therefore it contains the whole tropical linear space of $p|J$.
It remains only to note that $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is a vector of $p|J$: for
each valuated circuit of $p|J$, indexed by a set
$D\in\binom{J}{a+1}$, the orthogonality relation
\[
 \min_{e\in D}\{p(I\cup e)+p(D\setminus e)\}
\]
is exactly a Plucker relation for $p$.  Hence $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is
orthogonal to $c$, which is the desired arbitrary incidence relation.
\end{proof}

We also use Wang's codimension-one theorem in the following precise
form.  In Wang's terminology the ``three-term incidence relations''
consist of degenerate, parallel, and concurrent hyperplane relations;
the displayed tropical minimum below is only the concurrent relation.

\begin{proposition}[elementary quotient test]\label{prop:elementarytest}
Let $\eta$ be a rank $d$ valuated matroid and let
$\theta:\binom{E}{d-1}\to{\mathbb R}\cup\{\infty\}$ be nonzero.  Suppose
that
\[
        \theta(A)=\infty\quad\hbox{whenever }A
        \hbox{ is dependent in the support matroid of }\eta              \tag{D}
\]
and, when $d\ge2$, for every $S\in\binom{E}{d-2}$ and distinct $x,y,z\notin S$,
\[
\min\{\theta(Sx)+\eta(Syz),\,
      \theta(Sy)+\eta(Sxz),\,
      \theta(Sz)+\eta(Sxy)\}                                      \tag{C}
\]
is attained at least twice.  Then $\theta$ is a valuated matroid and
$\theta\preceq\eta$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For $d=1$ this is the unique rank-zero quotient.  Let $d\ge2$.  By
Wang's Theorem 4.3 \cite{Wang}, $\Dr^1(\eta)$ is cut out by the
degenerate, parallel, and concurrent three-term incidence relations.
Condition (D) is exactly the degenerate part, and (C) contains the
concurrent part.  It remains only to see that the parallel part follows
from (C).  Write $A=Di$ and $B=Dj$ for independent $(d-1)$-sets in the
support matroid of $\eta$ with the same closure, and choose $k$ outside
that closure, so $Dik$ and $Djk$ are bases.  Applying (C) to
$D,i,j,k$, the third upper coordinate $\eta(Dij)$ is $\infty$, while
$\eta(Dik)$ and $\eta(Djk)$ are finite.  Hence
\[
        \theta(Di)+\eta(Djk)=\theta(Dj)+\eta(Dik),
\]
which is Wang's parallel hyperplane relation.  Thus all of Wang's
defining relations hold, so $\theta$ represents a codimension-one
tropical linear subspace of $\Trop(\eta)$.
\end{proof}

Finally, if $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ has rank $k$, we use its valuated Higgs
completion
\[
 h_j(B)=
 \begin{cases}
 \min\{\nu(C): B\subseteq C,\ |C|=k\}, & j\le k,\ |B|=j,\\
 \min\{\nu(C): C\subseteq B,\ |C|=k\}, & j\ge k,\ B\in\mathcal I_j(M),\\
 \infty, & j\ge k,\ B\notin\mathcal I_j(M).
 \end{cases}                                                \tag{1}
\]
Murota's truncation and relative elongation theorems \cite[Theorems 2.1 and 4.1]{Murota}, equivalently the
Dress--Terhalle well-layering construction \cite{DT}, give valuated matroids
$h_j$ and adjacent quotients $h_j\preceq h_{j+1}$.  The top layer is
projectively $\mu_M$: if $B'=B-e+f$ are adjacent $M$-bases and
$C\subseteq B$ minimizes (1), then either $e\notin C$ and $C\subseteq B'$,
or the incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $C-e$ and
upper set $B\cup f$ gives a $k$-subset of $B'$ of value at most
$\nu(C)$; by symmetry the two top-layer values are equal.

\section*{1. Flat-lattice duality and flat constancy}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:modular}
If $\cL(M)$ admits an order-reversing involution $X\mapsto X^\perp$,
then $\cL(M)$ is modular and $\rk(X^\perp)=r-\rk(X)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The anti-automorphism reverses maximal chains, so it sends rank $i$ to
rank $r-i$.  Applying it to the semimodular inequality for the geometric
lattice $\cL(M)$ gives the reverse inequality, hence equality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[flat constancy]\label{lem:flatconst}
Let $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k$.  If $B,B'$ are independent
$k$-subsets of $M$ with $\cl(B)=\cl(B')$, then $\nu(B)=\nu(B')$; and
$\nu(A)=\infty$ whenever $A$ is dependent in $M$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The support matroid of $\nu$ is an ordinary quotient of $M$, so its
bases are independent in $M$.  For $k>0$ it suffices to compare
$B=A\cup b$ and $B'=A\cup b'$ spanning the same rank $k$ flat.  Choose
one complement $C$ so that both $AbC$ and $Ab'C$ are $M$-bases.  In the
incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup\{b,b'\}\cup C$, the only finite $\mu_M$-terms are the
$b$- and $b'$-terms.  Thus $\nu(Ab)=\nu(Ab')$.
\end{proof}

Write $\bar\nu(X)$ for the common value on independent $k$-sets spanning
the rank $k$ flat $X$, and put $\bar\nu(X)=\infty$ if no basis of $\nu$
spans $X$.

\section*{2. The polar transform}

For a rank $k$ quotient $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ define
\[
 \nu^{\perp_M}(A)=
 \begin{cases}
   \bar\nu\bigl((\cl A)^\perp\bigr),
      & A\text{ independent in }M,\ |A|=r-k,\\
   \infty,&\text{otherwise.}
 \end{cases}                                                 \tag{2}
\]
This is projectively well-defined, and applying it twice recovers the
original flat coordinates.

\begin{lemma}[ordinary polar rank]\label{lem:ordinary}
Let $N$ be an ordinary quotient of $M$ of rank $k$, with rank function
$\rho_N$.  Then
\[
        \rho^\perp(A)=\rk_M(A)-k+\rho_N((\cl A)^\perp)          \tag{3}
\]
is a matroid rank function.  Its bases are precisely the finite support
of (2).  If $N_p$ is a quotient of $N_q$, then
$N_q^{\perp_M}$ is a quotient of $N_p^{\perp_M}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since $N$ is a quotient of $M$, $\rho_N$ is constant on $M$-closures:
if $e\in\cl_M(A)$, then the $N$-rank increment from $A$ to $Ae$ is at
most the corresponding $M$-increment, namely $0$.  First restrict (3) to
flats.  For flats $X\le Y$,
\[
\rho^\perp(Y)-\rho^\perp(X)
 =\rk(Y)-\rk(X)-\bigl(\rho_N(X^\perp)-\rho_N(Y^\perp)\bigr)
\]
is between $0$ and $\rk(Y)-\rk(X)$ by the strong-map increment
inequality, so $\rho^\perp$ is monotone with unit increments on flats.
For flats $X,Y$, modularity of $\rk_M$ and the identities
$(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp$ and
$(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp$ reduce submodularity of
$\rho^\perp$ to submodularity of $\rho_N$.

For arbitrary subsets, $\rho^\perp(A)=\rho^\perp(\cl_M A)$.  If
$X=\cl A$ and $Y=\cl B$, then
$\cl(A\cap B)\le X\wedge Y$ and $\cl(A\cup B)=X\vee Y$; by monotonicity
on flats, submodularity for $X,Y$ implies submodularity for $A,B$.
The unit-increment property also passes from flats to subsets.  Thus
(3) is a matroid rank function.  An $M$-independent set $A$ of size
$r-k$ has full $\rho^\perp$-rank exactly when
$\rho_N((\cl A)^\perp)=k$, and $M$-dependent sets cannot be bases; this
is precisely the finite support of (2).

If $N_p$ is a quotient of $N_q$, then on every interval the rank
increment of $N_p$ is at most that of $N_q$.  The displayed formula for
increments therefore reverses the inequality for the polar ranks, which
is exactly the strong-map criterion saying that $N_q^{\perp_M}$ is a
quotient of $N_p^{\perp_M}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[elementary opposition]\label{lem:elementary}
Let $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, with $\rk p=d$ and $\rk q=d+1$.  Then
$q^{\perp_M}$ and $p^{\perp_M}$ satisfy all adjacent three-term
relations of ranks $r-d-1$ and $r-d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix $A$ of size $r-d-2$ and distinct $x,y,z\notin A$.  Put
$U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(Axyz)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  If $A$ is
dependent, or if $\delta\le1$, each term contains a dependent set of the
required size and hence is $\infty$.

If $\delta=2$, let $G=W^\perp$ and $H=U^\perp$, of ranks $d$ and
$d+2$.  Whenever the term indexed by $x$ is finite, $Ayz$ spans $W$,
so its $p$-factor is the common value $\bar p(G)$; its $q$-factor is
$\bar q((\cl(Ax))^\perp)$, and similarly for $y,z$.  In the rank-two
interval $[U,W]$, a loop over $U$ makes all terms involving it
infinite, and two parallel elements give equal corresponding
$q$-coordinates.  Thus only the case of three non-parallel points over
$U$ needs proof.  Choose a basis $S$ of $G$, representatives
$a_x,a_y,a_z$ for the three dual rank-one flats over $G$ in $[G,H]$,
and a complement $C$ of $H$ to a basis of $M$.  In the incidence
relation for $q\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $S$ and upper set
$S\cup\{a_x,a_y,a_z\}\cup C$, the only finite $\mu_M$-terms are those
obtained by deleting one of $a_x,a_y,a_z$.  Hence the three displayed
$q$-coordinates have a non-unique minimum; adding the common
$\bar p(G)$ proves the transformed relation.

If $\delta=3$, then $x,y,z$ form a Boolean frame over $U$.  Let
$G=W^\perp$ and
\[
 P_x=(\cl(Ayz))^\perp,\quad P_y=(\cl(Axz))^\perp,\quad
 P_z=(\cl(Axy))^\perp .
\]
These are the three atoms over $G$ in the dual frame, and
$(\cl(Ax))^\perp=P_y\vee P_z$, with the analogous identities for
$y,z$.  Choose a basis $S$ of $G$ and representatives
$a_e\in P_e\setminus G$.  The adjacent incidence relation for
$p\preceq q$ applied to the lower set $S$ and to
$S\cup\{a_x,a_y,a_z\}$ is exactly the transformed three-term relation,
using flat constancy to identify the displayed coordinates.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:set}
The formula $\nu\mapsto\nu^{\perp_M}$ defines an involution of the set
underlying $\Dr(M)$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Insert $\nu$ in the complete flag (1).  We prove inductively that
\[
        h_r^{\perp_M}\preceq h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}\preceq\cdots
        \preceq h_0^{\perp_M}=\mu_M .
\]
After harmless scalar normalization, the right endpoint
$h_0^{\perp_M}=\mu_M$ is clear from (2).  If $r\le1$, there is
nothing more to check beyond the automatic rank-zero quotient.  Now assume $r\ge2$.
Suppose $0\le j\le r-2$ and $h_j^{\perp_M}$ has
already been proved valuated.  Since $h_j\preceq h_{j+1}$, Lemma
\ref{lem:ordinary} says that the ordinary support of
$h_{j+1}^{\perp_M}$ is an elementary quotient of the ordinary support of
$h_j^{\perp_M}$; in particular it satisfies the degenerate support
condition (D) relative to $h_j^{\perp_M}$.  Lemma
\ref{lem:elementary} gives the displayed concurrent relations (C) for
the pair $h_{j+1}^{\perp_M},h_j^{\perp_M}$.  Proposition
\ref{prop:elementarytest} then implies
$h_{j+1}^{\perp_M}\preceq h_j^{\perp_M}$.  This proves the induction
down to $h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}$; the last rank-zero quotient
$h_r^{\perp_M}\preceq h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}$ is automatic.  Hence $\nu^{\perp_M}$, one layer of the flag, lies in
$\Dr(M)$.  Involutivity follows from (2) and $(X^\perp)^\perp=X$.
\end{proof}

\section*{3. Order reversal}

\begin{lemma}[circuit minima]\label{lem:circuit}
Let $w\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k\ge1$, let $A$ be an independent
$(k-1)$-set of $M$, and let $C$ be a circuit of $M/A$ with $|C|\ge2$.
Then $\min_{i\in C}w(A\cup i)$ is attained at least twice.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $F=\cl(A\cup C)$.  For every $i\in C$, $A\cup(C-i)$ is independent
and spans $F$.  Choose one complement $D$ of $F$ to an $M$-basis.  In
the incidence relation for $w\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup C\cup D$, the finite $\mu_M$-terms are exactly those indexed
by $i\in C$.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:order}
If $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, then
\[
        q^{\perp_M}\preceq p^{\perp_M}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $\rk p=a$ and $\rk q=b$.  If $a=b$, the strong-map inequalities applied to
$\emptyset\subseteq A$ and to $A\subseteq E$ force the two ordinary
support rank functions to be equal.  Then $p$ and $q$ are projectively
equal: the equal-rank incidence relation on adjacent bases shows
$p(B)-q(B)$ is constant on the common basis graph.  If $a=0$, then $p^{\perp_M}=\mu_M$, so the
assertion is $q^{\perp_M}\preceq\mu_M$; if $b=r$, it is the vacuous
rank-zero lower quotient.  Hence assume $0<a<b<r$.

By Proposition \ref{prop:set} the polars are valuated matroids, and by
Lemma \ref{lem:ordinary} their ordinary supports are in quotient order.
By Criterion \ref{crit:local} it remains to prove nested incidence.
Fix $A\subseteq T$ with
$|A|=r-b-1$, $|T|=r-a+1$, set $B=T\setminus A$, and
$m=|B|=b-a+2$.  If $A$ is dependent all terms are $\infty$.  Otherwise
put $U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(T)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  A finite term
forces $T-i$ to be independent, hence $\delta\ge m-1$; since
$\delta\le m$, only two cases remain.

If $\delta=m$, then $T$ is independent over $U$.  Let $G=W^\perp$ and
$H=U^\perp$, of ranks $a-1$ and $b+1$.  The interval generated by $B$
is Boolean; after applying $^\perp$, the flats
$X_i=(\cl(T-i))^\perp$ are the atoms over $G$, and
$Y_i=(\cl(Ai))^\perp$ is the join of all $X_j$ with $j\ne i$.  Choose a
basis $S$ of $G$ and representatives $x_i\in X_i\setminus G$.  The
nested incidence relation for $p\preceq q$ with lower set $S$ and upper
set $S\cup\{x_i:i\in B\}$ is exactly the desired transformed relation.

If $\delta=m-1$, then $B$ has nullity one in $M/A$, hence a unique
circuit $C$.  A loop circuit gives only $\infty$ terms.  Otherwise, for
$i\notin C$ the set $T-i$ is dependent; for $i\in C$ it is independent
and spans $W$, so the $p^{\perp_M}(T-i)$ factor is the common value
$\bar p(W^\perp)$.  Lemma \ref{lem:circuit}, applied to
$w=q^{\perp_M}$ and the circuit $C$ of $M/A$, gives two minima among
the remaining $q^{\perp_M}(Ai)$ terms.  This proves nested incidence.
\end{proof}

\section*{4. Agreement with flats}

Let $F$ be a flat of rank $f$, and let $q_F$ be the trivial rank
$r-f$ valuated matroid of $M/F\oplus U_{0,F}$.  For an independent
$(r-f)$-set $B$ with $Z=\cl(B)$,
\[
        q_F(B)<\infty\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Z\vee F=\hat1.
\]
For an independent $f$-set $A$ with $Y=\cl(A)$, formula (2) gives
\[
 q_F^{\perp_M}(A)<\infty
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Y^\perp\vee F=\hat1 .
\]
By modularity, since $\rk(Y^\perp)=r-f$ and $\rk F=f$, this is
equivalent to $Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat0$.  Applying the anti-automorphism
gives $Y\vee F^\perp=\hat1$, the basis condition for
$M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}$.  The finite values are zero on both
sides.  Thus the polar transform sends the flat $F$ to $F^\perp$.

Combining Proposition \ref{prop:set}, Theorem \ref{thm:order}, and this
flat computation, the map
\[
        \Phi([\nu])=[\nu^{\perp_M}]
\]
is an order-reversing involution of $\Dr(M)$ extending the given
order-reversing involution of $\cL(M)$.

\begin{center}
{\bf Hook coefficients in the universal Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
 R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\,
/\,\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle,
\]
where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by simultaneously permuting the subscript \(i\) in all
\(m\) sets of variables.  Thus the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the stable
multiplicities of the polynomial \(GL_m\)-irreducibles \(S_\lambda(\mathbb C^m)\).

\section*{The combinatorial model}

For \(r,k\ge 1\), let \(\OP_{r,k}\) be the set of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\) into \(k\) nonempty blocks.  An inversion of
\(\sigma\) is a pair \((i,j)\) such that
\[
        i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m .
\]
Let \(\inv(\sigma)\) be the number of such pairs.  When \(k=r\), this is the
usual inversion number of the permutation obtained by reading the singleton
blocks from left to right.

\begin{theorem}
Let \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) be a hook, with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\).  Then
\(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\), for some \(2\le r\le n\), such that
\[
        r-k=b+1,\qquad r\hbox{ is not a singleton block of }\sigma,\qquad
        \inv(\sigma)=a-1 .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\,q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\ \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\
                  r\text{ not singleton in }\sigma}}
        q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
The empty partition coefficient is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We use two standard inputs.  First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the
universal Schur coefficients for diagonal coinvariants can be read from the
coinvariant ring with one bosonic alphabet and one fermionic alphabet,
\[
 SR_n=\bigl(\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes
          \bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\bigr)
/\langle(\mathbb C[x]\otimes\bigwedge\theta)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
More explicitly, the supersymmetric Frobenius/character expansion has the same
universal multiplicity modules as the purely bosonic expansion; after applying
the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor one obtains exactly
the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appearing in the question.  Therefore
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) denotes the super Schur polynomial in one even variable
\(q\) and one odd variable \(z\).  With one even and one odd variable,
\(s_\lambda(q/z)\) vanishes unless \(\lambda\) lies in the \((1,1)\)-hook, i.e.
unless \(\lambda\) is a hook, and
\[
        s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
        =q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Thus
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
              c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b .          \tag{1}
\]

Second, the Hilbert series of \(SR_n\), due to Rhoades--Wilson, is
\[
        H_n(q,z):=\Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,\Stir_q(n,k),                 \tag{2}
\]
where \([k]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{k-1}\), \([k]!_q=[k]_q[k-1]_q\cdots[1]_q\), and
\[
        \Stir_q(r,k)=\Stir_q(r-1,k-1)+[k]_q\Stir_q(r-1,k).
\]
The boundary conditions are
\(\Stir_q(0,0)=1\), \(\Stir_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), and
\(\Stir_q(r,k)=0\) outside \(0\le k\le r\).  Put
\(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\Stir_q(r,k)\).  Then \(F_{r,k}=0\) outside
\(1\le k\le r\), except for the harmless convention \(F_{0,0}=1\), and
\[
        F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\) in (2), we get, for \(n\ge2\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_n(q,z)
&=\sum_{k=1}^{n}z^{n-k}\bigl([k]_qF_{n-1,k-1}
                         +[k]_qF_{n-1,k}\bigr)\\
&=H_{n-1}(q,z)
 +(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_1(q,z)=1\), iteration gives
\[
        \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
        =
        \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}
             [k]_q\,F_{r,k}\,z^{r-k}.                    \tag{3}
\]

It remains to identify the right side of (3).  The polynomial \(F_{r,k}\) is
the inversion enumerator of ordered set partitions:
\[
        F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in \OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}.                \tag{4}
\]
Indeed, both sides satisfy the same recurrence.  To see this directly, insert
the largest letter \(r\) into an ordered partition of \([r-1]\).  To obtain
\(k\) final blocks, either insert \(r\) as a singleton block into one of the
\(k\) possible final block positions, or insert \(r\) into one of the \(k\)
existing blocks.  If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new
inversions involving \(r\) are exactly the minima of the blocks to its right, so
their number is \(k-i\).  Summing over \(i=1,\ldots,k\) contributes
\([k]_q\) in each of the two cases, giving the recurrence above and the initial
condition.

Now restrict this insertion argument to insertions of \(r+1\) into an existing
block of an ordered partition \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\).  The result is precisely an
ordered partition \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton,
and the extra inversion contribution is again one of \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\).
Hence
\[
 [k]_qF_{r,k}
 =
 \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
      q^{\inv(\sigma)} .
\]
Substituting this into (3), and replacing \(r+1\) by \(r\), gives exactly
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\r\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) proves the claimed interpretation.
\end{proof}

\title{Proper proximality of irreducible graph-product von Neumann algebras}
\author{}
\date{}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

Irreducible means not a non-trivial graph join; equivalently the complement
graph \(\Gamma^c\) is connected.  All traces are faithful normal traces.  For
\(U\subset V\), \(M_U\) denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph and
\(E_U:M\to M_U\) its canonical trace-preserving expectation.

We use the notation of Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli--Peterson \cite{DKEP}.
For a finite algebra \(N\), put \(A_N=\mathbb B(L^2N)\) and
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*,
\]
where \(\sharp_J\) means simultaneous normality for the \(N\)- and
\(JNJ\)-bimodule structures.  If \(X\) is an \(N\)-boundary piece, \(K_X(N)\)
is DKEP's compact hereditary algebra and \(q_X\in\cA_N\) is the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\).  For \(X=\mathbb K(L^2N)\) write \(q_0\).  Set
\[
 \widetilde S_X(N)=\{T\in\cA_N:[T,a]\in q_X\cA_Nq_X\hbox{ for all }
 a\in JNJ\}.
\]
For a separable finite factor, DKEP Lemma 8.5 says that proper proximality
relative to \(X\) is equivalent to the absence of an \(N\)-central state on
\(\widetilde S_X(N)\) whose restriction to \(N\) is the trace.  Equivalently
one may rule out \(N\)-central states whose restriction is normal, since a
normal central state on a finite factor is the trace after normalization.

For a bounded vector \(\xi\) in an \(M\)-\(N\) correspondence, \(T_\xi\) denotes
DKEP's coefficient operator \(L^2M\to L^2N\); if \(\eta\) is bounded in an
\(N\)-\(P\) correspondence, then
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes_N\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi,\qquad
        T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^* .                                    \tag{1}
\]

\section*{Boundary supports and coefficients}

We use DKEP's ordinary-bidual realization of the normal bidual.  Thus
\(\cA_N\) is viewed as a von Neumann subalgebra of \(A_N^{**}\); its unit is
denoted by \(p_N\).  Products of support projections are computed inside the
honest von Neumann algebra \(A_N^{**}\), not by multiplying the
non-multiplicative canonical images of concrete operators.

\begin{lemma}[ordinary support bookkeeping]\label{lem:support}
Let \(Y\subset A_N\) be a hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra invariant under the
multiplier actions of \(N\) and \(JNJ\), and assume that the induced
representations \(N,JNJ\to M(Y)\) are faithful.  Let \(r_Y\in A_N^{**}\) be
the ordinary open support projection of \(Y\).  Then \(r_Y\) commutes with
\(p_N\), and the support in \(\cA_N\) of the included corner
\((Y^{\sharp_J})^*\) is \(p_Nr_Y\).  Every contractive approximate unit of
\(Y\) converges weak* in \(A_N^{**}\) to \(r_Y\).

If \(X\) is one of the boundary pieces used below and \(\rho_X\) denotes the
ordinary support of \(K_X(N)\), while \(r_X\) denotes the ordinary support of
\(X\), then
\[
        q_X=p_Nr_X=p_N\rho_X .                                    \tag{2}
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\) be DKEP's one-normal support projections in
\(A_N^{**}\), for the \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-module structures respectively.  In
the one-normal construction \(p_\ell\in N^{**}\) and
\(p_r\in (JNJ)^{**}\).  Since the concrete algebras \(N\) and \(JNJ\) commute
in \(A_N\), their bidual images commute in \(A_N^{**}\): approximate elements
of the biduals weak* by bounded nets from the concrete algebras and use
separate weak* continuity of multiplication.  Hence \(p_\ell p_r=p_rp_\ell\).

The one-normal support paragraph preceding DKEP Remark 2.8 identifies
\((A_N^{\sharp_N})^*=p_\ell A_N^{**}p_\ell\) and
\((A_N^{\sharp_{JNJ}})^*=p_rA_N^{**}p_r\).  DKEP Proposition 2.10 identifies
the simultaneous normal bidual with their intersection inside \(A_N^{**}\).
For commuting projections \(e,f\) in a von Neumann algebra,
\(eRe\cap fRf=(ef)R(ef)\); therefore
\[
        \cA_N=(A_N^{\sharp_J})^*=p_NA_N^{**}p_N,\qquad
        p_N=p_\ell p_r .                                          \tag{3}
\]

Apply the same one-normal support statement to \(Y\) for each of the two
normal structures.  The faithfulness hypothesis is exactly the hypothesis
needed there for the multiplier actions on \(Y\).  Thus \(r_Y\) commutes with
both \(p_\ell\) and \(p_r\), and the two one-normal biduals of \(Y\) are
\(r_Yp_\ell A_N^{**}p_\ell r_Y\) and \(r_Yp_rA_N^{**}p_rr_Y\).  Applying
Proposition 2.10 to the inclusion \(Y\subset A_N\) and using the elementary
intersection formula once more gives
\[
        (Y^{\sharp_J})^*
        = r_Yp_NA_N^{**}p_Nr_Y
        = (p_Nr_Y)\,\cA_N\,(p_Nr_Y).
\]
Thus its support projection in \(\cA_N\) is \(p_Nr_Y\).  The weak* convergence
of approximate units to \(r_Y\) is the usual open-support property in the
ordinary bidual.

It remains to compare \(X\) with \(K_X(N)\).  For the subalgebra boundary
pieces used below the faithfulness assumption is automatic: if, for example,
\(a e_B=0\), then \(E_B(a^*a)=0\), hence \(a=0\), and the right action is
similar; the same holds for \(K_X(N)\) since \(K(L^2N)\subset X\subset K_X(N)\).
By the first part, the normal supports of \(X\) and \(K_X(N)\) are \(p_Nr_X\)
and \(p_N\rho_X\).  We show that their simultaneous-normal annihilators
coincide.

Let \(\phi\in A_N^{\sharp_J}\) vanish on \(X\).  DKEP Proposition 3.6 gives
\(K_X(N)\subset K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\), where \(K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is the
\(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(X\).  DKEP Theorem 5.6, applied with the
same boundary piece on both sides, says that every
\(T\in K_X^{\infty,1}(N)\) is killed by every simultaneous-normal functional
which vanishes on \(X A_N X\).  Since \(X\) is hereditary, \(X A_N X\subset X\);
hence \(\phi(T)=0\) for all \(T\in K_X(N)\).  The reverse annihilator inclusion
is immediate from \(X\subset K_X(N)\), which is part of DKEP's construction.
Equal preannihilators give the same weak* closed corner in \(\cA_N\), hence the
same support projection.  Since \(q_X\) is by definition the support of
\((K_X(N)^{\sharp_J})^*\), (2) follows.
\end{proof}

For an expected inclusion \(B\subset N\), put \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) and
\[
        \cH_B=H\otimes_B\overline H
             ={}_NL^2N_B\otimes_B{}_BL^2N_N .
\]
Let \(\mathcal E_B=\{T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}:\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\}\).

\begin{lemma}[subalgebra fusion]\label{lem:subalg}
The boundary piece \(X_B\) is hereditarily generated by the positive
operators \(s^*s\), \(s\in\mathcal E_B\), equivalently by
\(T_\alpha^*T_\alpha\) with \(\alpha\) an elementary bounded tensor in
\(\cH_B\).  Moreover \(\cH_B\cong\overline{\cH_B}\), and every coefficient
operator \(T_\alpha\) of a bounded vector in any Hilbert direct sum of copies
of \(\cH_B\) belongs to \(K_{X_B}(N)\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
DKEP Example 5.11 applied to \(H={}_NL^2N_B\) says that \(X_B\) is the
hereditary algebra generated by \(T_\xi^*T_\eta\), \(\xi,\eta\in H_{\rm b}\).
By (1), \(T_{\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi}=T_{\bar\xi}T_\eta=T_\xi^*T_\eta\).

Let \(Y\) be the hereditary algebra generated by \(\{s^*s:s\in\mathcal E_B\}\).
Since \(\mathcal E_B\) is self-adjoint, \(Y\) contains \(s^*s\) and \(ss^*\).
If \(r\) is the ordinary support of \(Y\), then
\((1-r)s^*s(1-r)=0\) and \((1-r)ss^*(1-r)=0\); hence \((1-r)s=s(1-r)=0\).
Thus \(s=rsr\in rA_N^{**}r\cap A_N=Y\).  So all generators
\(T_\xi^*T_\eta\) of \(X_B\) lie in \(Y\), and the reverse inclusion is
immediate.

The conjugation \(\overline{\xi\otimes_B\bar\eta}\mapsto
\eta\otimes_B\bar\xi\) gives \(\overline{\cH_B}\cong\cH_B\).  Let
\(\mathcal C\) be the elementary bounded tensors in \(\cH_B\).  They are
\(N\)-\(N\) cyclic, and \(T_\alpha\in X_B\subset X_BA_NX_B\) for
\(\alpha\in\mathcal C\).  Hence DKEP Theorem 5.10(1) applies, so \(\cH_B\) is
mixing relative to \(X_B\times X_B\).  For a Hilbert direct sum
\(\bigoplus_{j\in J}\cH_B\), take the vectors of \(\mathcal C\) placed in one
summand.  Their \(N\)-\(N\) span contains the finite-support algebraic direct
sum, hence is dense, and the coefficient operator of such a one-summand vector
is exactly the coefficient operator above (zero against the other summands).
Thus Theorem 5.10(1) holds for the whole direct sum.  Theorem 5.10(3), followed
by Proposition 5.9, gives \(T_\alpha\in K_{X_B,X_B}(N,N)=K_{X_B}(N)A_N
K_{X_B}(N)\subset K_{X_B}(N)\) for every bounded vector \(\alpha\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[finite fusion calculus]\label{lem:fusion}
Let \(X\) be a boundary piece for \(N\), with \(N,JNJ\subset M(X)\).  Finite
Connes fusions, conjugates, and Hilbert direct sums of \(N\)-\(N\)
correspondences which are mixing relative to \(X\times X\) are again mixing
relative to \(X\times X\).  Consequently, if \(K\) is any such finite-fusion
correspondence and \(\xi\in K\) is bounded, then \(T_\xi\in K_X(N)\).

For \(U\subset V\), put \(H_U=L^2M\otimes_{M_U}L^2M\).  Let \(\cT_U\) be the
linear span of all \(T_\xi\), where \(\xi\) is a bounded vector in a finite
direct sum of non-empty finite fusions of \(H_U\) and \(\overline{H_U}\).
Then \(\cT_U\) is a \(*\)-algebra, and \(X_{M_U}\) has a contractive
approximate unit in the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
For a mixing correspondence \(H\), Theorem 5.10 says that
\[
  \mathcal C_H=\{\xi\in H_{\rm b}: T_\xi\in X A_N X\}
\]
is \(N\)-\(N\) cyclic.  Direct sums are handled by placing the cyclic vectors
in one summand; their span contains the finite-support algebraic sum and is
dense, while the coefficient condition is unchanged.  If \(H\) and \(K\) are
mixing and \(\xi\in\mathcal C_H,\eta\in\mathcal C_K\), then \(\xi\otimes_N\eta\)
is bounded, such elementary tensors are cyclic for \(H\otimes_NK\), and
\[
        T_{\xi\otimes\eta}=T_\eta T_\xi\in (X A_N X)(X A_N X)\subset X A_N X,
\]
because \(X\) is hereditary.  Thus the cyclic-vector criterion proves mixing
for fusions.  For the conjugate correspondence, the cyclic vectors are
\(\bar\xi\) and \(T_{\bar\xi}=T_\xi^*\in X A_NX\).  Iterating gives the first
assertion.  Applying Theorem 5.10(3) and Proposition 5.9 to any bounded vector
\(\xi\) in such a correspondence gives
\[
        T_\xi\in K_{X,X}(N,N)=K_X(N)A_NK_X(N)\subset K_X(N).
\]

The algebra property of \(\cT_U\) follows term by term from (1) with the order
convention \(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\): the product of two coefficient
operators is the coefficient of the corresponding fused bounded vector, and the
adjoint is the coefficient of the conjugate vector.  Since \(\cT_U\) is defined
as a linear span, this proves closure under products and adjoints.  By Lemma
\ref{lem:subalg}, \(X_{M_U}\) is hereditarily generated
by \(T_\xi^*T_\xi\) for elementary tensors in \(H_U\), and these \(T_\xi\)'s
belong to \(\cT_U\).  If \(s\) is a finite positive sum of such generators,
then \(g_\varepsilon(s)=s(s+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is in the hereditary algebra
generated by the summands.  Polynomials in \(s\) with zero constant term
approximate \(g_\varepsilon(s)^{1/2}\), so \(g_\varepsilon(s)\) lies in the
norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_U\}\).  The usual net over \(s\) and
\(\varepsilon\downarrow0\) is a contractive approximate unit.
\end{proof}

If \(Z\subset U\), then \(e_{M_Z}\le e_{M_U}\); hence
\(X_{M_Z}\subset X_{M_U}\) and \(K_{X_{M_Z}}(M)\subset K_{X_{M_U}}(M)\).

\begin{lemma}[intersection of boundary supports]\label{lem:intersection}
Let \(q_U=q_{X_{M_U}}\).  For \(U,W\subset V\), with \(I=U\cap W\),
\[
        0\le q_Wq_Uq_W\le q_I .
\]
Consequently, if \(I=\cap_{i=1}^m U_i\), then
\[
q_{U_m}q_{U_{m-1}}\cdots q_{U_1}q_{U_2}\cdots q_{U_{m-1}}q_{U_m}
   \in q_I\cA_Mq_I .
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The graph-product bimodule decomposition and fusion rule of
\cite[Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5]{CDHJKN} give
\[
        H_A\otimes_M H_B\cong
        \bigoplus_{C\subset A\cap B}H_C^{\oplus m(A,B,C)} .        \tag{3}
\]
Since \(H_A\cong\overline{H_A}\), induction shows that a finite fusion whose
labels are \(A_1,\ldots,A_n\) decomposes into a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with
\(C\subset A_1\cap\cdots\cap A_n\).  The implementing unitary is
\(M\)-bimodular, so bounded vectors remain bounded.

Let \(\mathcal P_R\) be the norm closure of \(\{t^*t:t\in\cT_R\}\).  We claim
that
\[
        a\,b\,c\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)             \tag{4}
\]
whenever \(a,c\in\mathcal P_W\) and \(b\in\mathcal P_U\).  It is enough to
consider \(a=t_1^*t_1\), \(b=s^*s\), \(c=t_2^*t_2\).  Write
\(t_j\) and \(s\) as finite sums of coefficient operators.  A summand in the
expansion of \(t_1^*t_1s^*st_2^*t_2\) is
\[
T_{\alpha_1}^*T_{\alpha_2}T_{\beta_1}^*T_{\beta_2}
T_{\alpha_3}^*T_{\alpha_4},
\]
where each \(\alpha_i\) is a bounded vector in a finite direct sum of fusions
with label \(W\), and each \(\beta_i\) is similarly labelled by \(U\).  Replacing
\(T_\alpha^*\) by \(T_{\bar\alpha}\) and using
\(T_\eta T_\xi=T_{\xi\otimes\eta}\), the displayed product is the coefficient
operator of
\[
 \zeta:=\alpha_4\otimes\bar\alpha_3\otimes\beta_2\otimes\bar\beta_1
 \otimes\alpha_2\otimes\bar\alpha_1
\]
(with finite direct sums distributed over the tensor products).  This is a
bounded vector in a finite fusion whose label list contains both \(W\) and
\(U\).  By (3), it is carried by an \(M\)-bimodular unitary to a bounded vector
in a direct sum of \(H_C\)'s with \(C\subset I\); its coefficient operator is
unchanged under this identification.  For each such \(C\), Lemma
\ref{lem:subalg} gives mixing, hence coefficient compactness, relative to
\(X_{M_C}\).  Since \(X_{M_C}\subset X_{M_I}\), the same cyclic vectors are good
for \(X_{M_I}\); the direct-sum argument in Lemma \ref{lem:fusion} therefore
puts the coefficient of the whole direct sum in \(K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\).  Linearity
and norm closure prove (4).

Let \(A=\mathbb B(L^2M)\), let \(p=p_M\) be the unit of \(\cA_M\) in
\(A^{**}\), let \(r_R\) be the ordinary support of \(X_{M_R}\), and let
\(\rho_R\) be the ordinary support of \(K_{X_{M_R}}(M)\).  Lemma
\ref{lem:support} gives
\[
        q_R=pr_R=p\rho_R,\qquad [p,r_R]=[p,\rho_R]=0 .              \tag{5}
\]
Choose contractive approximate units
\(h_\alpha\subset\mathcal P_W\) for \(X_{M_W}\) and
\(k_\beta\subset\mathcal P_U\) for \(X_{M_U}\).  In \(A^{**}\),
\(h_\alpha\to r_W\) and \(k_\beta\to r_U\) weak*.  By (4),
\(h_\alpha k_\beta h_\gamma\in K_{X_{M_I}}(M)\subset
\rho_IA^{**}\rho_I\).  The corner \(\rho_IA^{**}\rho_I\) is weak* closed, and
multiplication in \(A^{**}\) is separately weak* continuous; taking the three
successive limits gives
\[
        r_W r_U r_W\in \rho_I A^{**}\rho_I .
\]
This operator is a positive contraction, hence \(r_Wr_Ur_W\le \rho_I\).
Compressing only now to the simultaneous normal bidual, (5) yields
\[
 q_Wq_Uq_W
 =(pr_Wp)(pr_Up)(pr_Wp)
 =p\,r_Wr_Ur_W\,p
 \le p\rho_Ip=q_I .
\]

The palindromic assertion follows by induction.  If
\(R_{m-1}\le q_{\cap_{i<m}U_i}\), then
\[
q_{U_m}R_{m-1}q_{U_m}
 \le q_{U_m}q_{\cap_{i<m}U_i}q_{U_m}
 \le q_{\cap_iU_i},
\]
using the two-set case for the last inequality.
\end{proof}

\section*{From finitely many relative boundaries to the compact boundary}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:finite}
Let \(N\) be a separable non-amenable finite factor.  Let
\(X_1,\ldots,X_m\) be boundary pieces, \(q_i=q_{X_i}\), and suppose
\[
        R:=q_mq_{m-1}\cdots q_1q_2\cdots q_{m-1}q_m\in q_0\cA_Nq_0. \tag{6}
\]
If \(N\) is properly proximal relative to each \(X_i\), then \(N\) is properly
proximal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume not.  DKEP Lemma 8.5 gives an \(N\)-central state \(\omega\) on
\(\widetilde S(N)=\widetilde S_{\mathbb K(L^2N)}(N)\) with
\(\omega|_N=\tau\).  Fix \(i\) and put \(q=q_i\).  Since \(q\) commutes with
\(N\) and \(JNJ\),
\[
 [q^\perp yq^\perp,a]=q^\perp[y,a]q^\perp=0,\qquad a\in JNJ,
\]
for \(y\in\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\); hence \(q^\perp yq^\perp\in\widetilde
S(N)\).  If \(\omega(q^\perp)>0\), then
\(y\mapsto \omega(q^\perp yq^\perp)/\omega(q^\perp)\) is an \(N\)-central state
on \(\widetilde S_{X_i}(N)\).  On \(N_+\) it is dominated by
\(\omega(q^\perp)^{-1}\tau\), hence is normal; its normalization and centrality
make its restriction the unique normal trace on the factor \(N\), contradicting
relative proper proximality.  Thus \(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) for all \(i\).

Each \(q_i\), \(q_0\), and the palindromic product \(R\) commute with \(JNJ\),
so they lie in \(\widetilde S(N)\); their values below are therefore independent
of any extension.  Extend \(\omega\) to a state on the \(C^*\)-algebra
\(\cA_N\) only for the following GNS projection argument; the extension is not
claimed to be \(N\)-central.  In its GNS representation, the equality
\(\omega(q_i^\perp)=0\) implies \(q_i\) fixes the cyclic vector.  Hence
\(\omega(R)=\|(q_1q_2\cdots q_m)\xi_\omega\|^2=1\).  Since \(R\) is a positive
contraction in \(q_0\cA_Nq_0\), \(R\le q_0\), and so \(\omega(q_0)=1\).

DKEP Lemma 8.5 provides the canonical \(N\)- and \(JNJ\)-bimodular u.c.p. map
\[
        \Theta_0:\mathbb B(L^2N)\to q_0\cA_Nq_0,\qquad
        \Theta_0(a)=aq_0\quad(a\in N).
\]
Then \(\psi(T)=\omega(\Theta_0(T))\) is an \(N\)-central state on
\(\mathbb B(L^2N)\).  Since \(q_0\) fixes the GNS cyclic vector,
\(\psi(a)=\omega(aq_0)=\omega(a)=\tau(a)\) for \(a\in N\).  This Connes
hypertrace contradicts non-amenability of \(N\).
\end{proof}

\section*{A relative Bass--Serre paradox}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:BS}
Let \(N=P*_{B}Q\) with trace-preserving expectations and \(Q=B\bbarotimes A\).
Assume that \(A\) has a trace-zero unitary \(a\), and that \(P\) has unitaries
\(w_1,w_2\) satisfying
\[
        E_B(w_i)=0,\qquad E_B(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
Then \(N\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Let \(H_P=L^2P\ominus L^2B\) and \(H_Q=L^2Q\ominus L^2B\).  The AFP normal form is
\[
 L^2N=L^2B\oplus\!\!\bigoplus_{\substack{n\ge1,\ i_j\in\{P,Q\}\\
 i_j\ne i_{j+1}}}
 H_{i_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B H_{i_n}.
\]
Let \(p\) and \(q\) project onto the summands whose first letter is \(P\),
respectively \(Q\); then \(1=e_B+p+q\).  Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves
the first letter.  Let \(x\in P\ominus B\).  On \(L^2B\), \(JxJ\) creates a
first-letter \(P\) vector.  On a reduced word with first letter \(Q\), right
multiplication by \(x^*\) either appends or merges at the last letter, but the
first letter remains \(Q\) and no \(L^2B\)-part is created.  On a first-letter
\(P\) word the first letter remains \(P\), except that a length-one word may
produce an \(L^2B\)-component.  Hence
\[
 [p,JxJ]=(1-e_B)JxJe_B-e_BJxJ(1-e_B),\qquad [q,JxJ]=0.             \tag{7}
\]
The symmetric formula holds with \(P,p\) and \(Q,q\) interchanged for
\(x\in Q\ominus B\).  Since \(E_B(x)=0\), \(e_BJxJe_B=0\); hence the two terms
in (7) are just right translates of \(e_B\) (up to sign), so they lie in
\(X_B\subset K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(N)\).

We now translate DKEP Lemma 6.1.  For a fixed operator \(T\), the set of
\(x\in N\) such that both \([T,JxJ]\) and \([T,Jx^*J]\) lie in
\(K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(N)\) is a von Neumann algebra; this is Lemma 6.1 applied
to \(B(L^2N)\) with DKEP's right-action convention
\(x\cdot T\cdot y=Jy^*JTJx^*J\).  For \(T=p\) and \(T=q\) this von Neumann
algebra contains \(B\), \(P\ominus B\), and \(Q\ominus B\), hence contains the
von Neumann algebra generated by \(P\) and \(Q\).  Thus \(p,q\in S_{X_B}(N)\).

It remains, by DKEP Theorem 6.2, to rule out an \(N\)-central state
\(\varphi\) on \(S_{X_B}(N)\) with normal restriction to \(N\).  For
\(\eta\in qL^2N\), \(w_i\eta\) is a reduced vector with first letter \(P\).
Moreover \(q\,w_1^*w_2\,q=0\): if a reduced vector begins with \(Q\), left
multiplication by the centered \(P\)-letter \(w_1^*w_2\) produces a vector
beginning with \(P\).  Thus
\[
        w_1qw_1^*+w_2qw_2^*\le p .
\]
Likewise \(a\in Q\ominus B\) and left multiplication by \(a\) sends every
first-letter \(P\) reduced word into a first-letter \(Q\) word, so
\[
        a p a^*\le q .
\]
Since \(S_{X_B}(N)\) is an \(N\)-bimodule and \(\varphi\) is \(N\)-central,
these inequalities give \(2\varphi(q)\le\varphi(p)\le\varphi(q)\), hence
\(\varphi(p)=\varphi(q)=0\).

The unitary \(h=w_1a\) is \(B\)-Haar: every non-zero power is an alternating
reduced word in centered \(P\)- and \(Q\)-letters, so \(E_B(h^n)=0\) for
\(n\ne0\).  For \(m\ne n\),
\[
h^ne_Bh^{-n}h^me_Bh^{-m}
 =h^n e_B h^{m-n}e_Bh^{-m}=h^nE_B(h^{m-n})e_Bh^{-m}=0 .
\]
The projections \(h^ne_Bh^{-n}\) are pairwise orthogonal and, by centrality,
have the same \(\varphi\)-value; hence \(\varphi(e_B)=0\).  This contradicts
\(1=\varphi(e_B+p+q)\).  By DKEP Theorem 6.2 this is precisely proper
proximality relative to \(X_B\) (and, in the factor case used below, agrees
with the normal-bidual criterion from Lemma 8.5).
\end{proof}

\section*{Application to graph products}

Fix \(v\in V\) and put \(L_v=\Lk_\Gamma(v)\).  The graph-product reduced-word
model, or \cite{CF17}, gives the standard amalgamated free product
decomposition
\[
        M\cong M_{V\setminus\{v\}}*_{M_{L_v}}
        (M_{L_v}\bbarotimes M_v).                                \tag{8}
\]
Let \(C(v)=\{r\ne v:r\not\sim_\Gamma v\}\).  Since \(\Gamma^c\) is connected,
\(C(v)\ne\emptyset\).  If \(C(v)\) contains distinct \(r,t\), choose trace-zero
unitaries \(u_r,u_t\) and set \(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_t\).  Then \(w_i\notin M_{L_v}\),
and the reduced-word expectation onto \(M_{L_v}\) kills \(w_i\) and
\(w_1^*w_2\).  If \(C(v)=\{r\}\), connectedness of \(\Gamma^c\) and
\(|V|\ge3\) give \(s\in L_v\) with \(s\not\sim_\Gamma r\); otherwise
\(\{v,r\}\) would be a component of \(\Gamma^c\).  Set
\(w_1=u_r,w_2=u_su_r\).  Here \(E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1)=0\),
\(E_{M_{L_v}}(w_2)=u_sE_{M_{L_v}}(u_r)=0\), and
\(w_1^*w_2=u_r^*u_su_r\) is a reduced word containing the outside vertex \(r\),
so its expectation onto \(M_{L_v}\) is \(0\).  Thus in all cases
\[
        E_{M_{L_v}}(w_i)=0,\qquad E_{M_{L_v}}(w_1^*w_2)=0 .
\]
A trace-zero unitary in \(M_v\) supplies the unitary \(a\) in (8), so
Proposition \ref{prop:BS} shows that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\(X_{M_{L_v}}\) for every \(v\).

Enumerate \(V=\{v_1,\ldots,v_m\}\).  Since no vertex belongs to its own link,
\(\cap_i L_{v_i}=\emptyset\).  Lemma \ref{lem:intersection} puts the associated
palindromic product in \(q_\emptyset\cA_Mq_\emptyset\).  Here
\(M_\emptyset=\C\), \(e_\C\) is the rank-one projection onto
\(\C\widehat1\), and DKEP Example 3.4 gives
\(X_{M_\emptyset}=\mathbb K(L^2M)\).

Finally, CDHJKN Theorem 2.4 implies that a join-irreducible graph product with
\(|V|\ge3\) and trace-zero vertex unitaries is a diffuse full factor.  A
diffuse amenable finite factor is the hyperfinite II\(_1\) factor and is not
full, so \(M\) is non-amenable.  It is separable because the graph is finite
and the vertices are separable.  Lemma \ref{lem:finite}, applied to the finite
family \(\{X_{M_{L_v}}:v\in V\}\), proves that \(M\) is properly proximal.
